27 of 36 (d₁,d₂) pairs work ⇒ 27/216 = 1/8.
die-to-number.solution: Only the last three digits matter: need $100d_1+10d_2+d_3\equiv4d_1+2d_2+d_3\equiv0\pmod 8$. Since $4d_1+2d_2$ is even, for each $(d_1,d_2)$ there's a valid $d_3\in\{1,\dots,6\}$ exactly when $4d_1+2d_2\not\equiv0\pmod8$, which holds for $27$ of the $36$ pairs (each giving one $d_3$). So $P=\tfrac{27}{216}=\tfrac18.$

hints: T,G,P IID Geom(1/2); sum (1/2)^{t+g+p} over t≤g≤p.
Collapse the geometric sums innermost-out.
coin-flipping-competition-ii.solution: With $T,G,P\sim\mathrm{Geom}(\tfrac12)$, $P(T\le G\le P)=\sum_{t\le g\le p}(\tfrac12)^{t+g+p}$. Summing over $p\ge g$ gives $(\tfrac12)^{g-1}$; then over $g\ge t$ gives $\tfrac83(\tfrac14)^t$; then over $t\ge1$: $\tfrac83\cdot\dfrac{1/8}{7/8}=\dfrac{8}{21}.$

hints: Deuce means each player has won 3 points.
Total 6 points, choose which 3 the first player won.
tennis-deuces.solution: Reaching $40$–$40$ means each player has won $3$ points, so $6$ points were played and we choose which $3$ the first player won: $\binom63=20.$

hints: Use an indicator for each pair of chords intersecting.
Four random points pair into chords; they cross with probability 1/3.
intersecting-chords.solution: Two chords come from $4$ random points; of the $3$ ways to pair $4$ points into two chords, exactly $1$ makes them cross, so each pair intersects with probability $\tfrac13$. By linearity over $\binom{10}{2}=45$ pairs, the expected number of intersections is $45\cdot\tfrac13=15.$

hints: Each stop maps x → x/4 + 7; apply it 3 times.
After 3 stops the count is (x+588)/64; need it integer with all stages integer.
x=52 works: 52→20→12→10, smallest final = 10.
ferry-stops.solution: Each stop sends $x\mapsto\tfrac x4+7$. After three stops the count is $\tfrac{x}{64}+\tfrac{147}{16}=\tfrac{x+588}{64}$. Taking $x=52$ keeps every stage integral: $52\to20\to12\to10$. The smallest possible final count is $10.$

hints: No rain = dry both days; use independence.
P(dry Sat)=0.6, P(dry Sun)=0.3.
Write N(15)=(N(15)−N(5))+N(5) and use independent increments.
Range=|X₁−X₂|, midpoint=(X₁+X₂)/2; the condition becomes max > 3·min.
P(max>3min)=2P(X₂>3X₁) by symmetry.
midrange.solution: The range is $O_2-O_1$ and the midpoint $\tfrac{X_1+X_2}{2}=\tfrac{O_1+O_2}{2}$; the condition $O_2-O_1>\tfrac{O_1+O_2}{2}$ simplifies to $O_2>3O_1$, i.e. $\max>3\min$. By symmetry $P=2P(X_2>3X_1)=2\int_0^\infty e^{-x}e^{-3x}dx=2\cdot\tfrac14=\tfrac12.$

hints: The replacement is a uniform card from the 48 not originally selected.
By symmetry that card is marginally uniform over the deck.
min-card.solution: The replacement card is drawn uniformly from the $48$ cards not initially selected. By symmetry over which $4$ were selected, the replacement is marginally a uniformly random card of the deck, so its expected rank is $\tfrac{1+2+\dots+13}{13}=7.$

hints: Expected doors opened to reach the first of 2 'money' doors among 7 is (7+1)/(2+1) (First-Ace).
= 8/3 openings, each costing x; fair means cost = $200.
fair-bounty-ii.solution: By the 'First Ace' argument, the $2$ money doors split the $5$ empties into $3$ gaps, so the expected number of doors opened to hit the first money door is $\tfrac{7+1}{2+1}=\tfrac83$. Fairness requires $x\cdot\tfrac83=200$, so $x=75.$

hints: Focus on the center sub-cube of the 3×3×3.
Its 6 faces are all interior, each requiring a separate cut (repositioning can't create two opposite faces in one cut).
So at least 6 cuts; 6 suffice.
cube-slice.solution: Consider the central $1\times1\times1$ cube. Each of its $6$ faces is an internal surface that must be produced by a distinct cut (no single planar cut can form two opposite faces of it, even with rearranging). Hence at least $6$ cuts are needed, and $6$ (two per axis) suffice.

hints: Mean of U(0,λ) is λ/2; mean of Exp(λ) is 1/λ.
Set λ/2 = 1/λ.
λ²=2.
x̄=0, ȳ=1.5. Compute S_xy and S_xx.
ols-review-i.solution: Here $\bar x=0,\bar y=1.5$. Then $S_{xy}=\sum x_i(y_i-\bar y)=-6$ and $S_{xx}=\sum x_i^2=10$, so $\hat\beta_1=-0.6$ and $\hat\beta_0=\bar y-\hat\beta_1\bar x=1.5$. Their sum is $1.5-0.6=0.9.$

hints: P(stop before 234x)=x; profit is −2x then, +x otherwise.
Expected profit = x(−2x)+(1−x)x = x−3x².
Maximize: 1−6x=0 ⇒ x=1/6.

modified-rng.solution: With $E[Y\mid X]=\tfrac{X+1}2$ and $\mathrm{Var}(Y\mid X)=\tfrac{(1-X)^2}{12}$: $E[Y]=E[\tfrac{X+1}2]=\tfrac34$, and $\mathrm{Var}(Y)=E\big[\tfrac{(1-X)^2}{12}\big]+\mathrm{Var}\big(\tfrac{X+1}2\big)=\tfrac1{36}+\tfrac1{48}=\tfrac{7}{144}$. Hence $\dfrac{E[Y]}{\mathrm{Var}(Y)}=\dfrac{3/4}{7/144}=\dfrac{108}{7}.$

hints: Each of the 10 tiles produces an elimination (first attempt wrong) independently w.p. 1/2.
Contestant i wins iff exactly i−1 eliminations occur: p_i = C(10,i−1)/2^10.
squid-game-ii.solution: Each tile causes exactly one elimination if its first guess is wrong (probability $\tfrac12$), independently across the $10$ tiles. Contestant $i$ wins iff there are exactly $i-1$ eliminations, so $p_i=\dfrac{\binom{10}{i-1}}{2^{10}}$. This is largest at $i-1=5$: $\dfrac{\binom{10}{5}}{1024}=\dfrac{252}{1024}=\dfrac{63}{256}.$

hints: Don't just remove 5 from the sample space — conditioning changes the distribution of N.
show-the-face.solution: Let $N$ be the roll of the first $6$. For both $N=n$ and '6 before 5', the first $n-1$ rolls must be in $\{1,2,3,4\}$: $P(N=n,\,6\text{ first})=(\tfrac46)^{n-1}\tfrac16$. Summing gives $P(6\text{ before }5)=\tfrac12$. Then $\mathbb{E}[N\mid 6\text{ before }5]=\dfrac{\sum_n n(\tfrac23)^{n-1}\tfrac16}{1/2}=\tfrac13\cdot\dfrac1{(1-2/3)^2}=3.$

hints: Unfold the cube into a flat net so the path becomes a straight line.
The opposite corner unfolds to a point 1 across and 2 along.
ant-in-a-hurry.solution: Unfolding two adjacent faces into a plane, the far corner lies at horizontal distance $2$ and vertical distance $1$ from the start, so the shortest surface path is the straight segment of length $\sqrt{1^2+2^2}=\sqrt5$. Thus $d^2=5.$

hints: Write Z=e^{iθ} with θ~U(0,2π).
Z²=e^{2iθ}; average it over the circle.
You need exactly 1 quart in each of the 7- and 5-quart containers simultaneously.
Repeatedly wasting full 7-quart pours reduces the barrel toward a remainder you can split.
Combine the bulk-wasting phase with the classic 7/5 water-pouring measure steps and count transactions.
Grandmother's age = 10x+y where x,y are the grandchildren's ages (digits).
x=8 gives y=5, so grandmother is 85.
family-ties.solution: Let the grandmother be $10x+y$ with grandchildren aged $x$ and $y$. Then $(10x+y)+x+y=98\Rightarrow11x+2y=98$. The digit solution is $x=8,y=5$, giving grandmother's age $85$ (and $85+8+5=98$).

hints: Borrow at the lower rate (3%) and lend at the higher rate (5%) — a rate arbitrage.
Split your $200 so you both borrow and lend, then compare the year-end cash flows.
The locked-in profit is the spread applied to the committed amount.

statistical-test-review-vii.solution: The rejection region is $\hat p\le0.2-1.645\sqrt{\tfrac{0.2\cdot0.8}{100}}=0.1342$. Under the alternative $p=0.15$, $\hat p\approx N(0.15,\tfrac{0.15\cdot0.85}{100})$ with sd $0.0357$. Then $\beta=P(\hat p>0.1342)=P\!\big(Z>\tfrac{0.1342-0.15}{0.0357}\big)=\Phi(0.4425)\approx0.671.$

hints: Intrinsic value of a call = max(S−K,0).
extrinsic-value-i.solution: The call is in-the-money: intrinsic $I=\max(5-2,0)=3$, extrinsic $E=4.7-3=1.7$. So $I^2+E^2=9+2.89=11.89.$

hints: Each try is uniform over 5 doors; freedom doors end, loop doors add distance and restart.
magic-doors.solution: Each attempt picks a uniform door. Summing distances: $E=\tfrac15(2+5)+\tfrac15(1+E)+\tfrac15(3+E)+\tfrac15(4+E)=\tfrac{15}5+\tfrac35E=3+\tfrac35E$, so $\tfrac25E=3\Rightarrow E=\tfrac{15}{2}.$

hints: Let light users get an offer with rate r; heavy 2r, non-users r/2.
prepped-offer.solution: Let a light user get an offer with probability $r$; then heavy $=2r$, non-user $=r/2$. $P(\text{offer})=0.25(2r)+0.30(r)+0.45(\tfrac r2)=1.025r$ and $P(\text{heavy}\cap\text{offer})=0.25(2r)=0.5r$. So $P(\text{heavy}\mid\text{offer})=\tfrac{0.5}{1.025}=\tfrac{20}{41}.$

hints: M(4)=E[e^{4X}]=E[(e^X)^4]; apply Jensen with the convex function t↦t⁴.
= 7⁴ = 2401, attained when X is constant.
existent-mgf.solution: Since $t\mapsto t^4$ is convex, Jensen gives $M(4)=\mathbb{E}[(e^X)^4]\ge(\mathbb{E}[e^X])^4=7^4=2401$, with equality when $X$ is constant. So the smallest possible value is $2401.$

hints: Bob loses unless Alice goes bankrupt twice; compute P(Alice bankrupt) each phase by gambler's ruin.
P(Alice loses)=(2/5)(5/8)=1/4 ⇒ P(Bob loses)=3/4.
rebuy-gambler.solution: Bob loses unless Alice is bankrupted twice. First phase (Alice $15$ of $25$ total): $P(\text{ruin})=1-\tfrac{15}{25}=\tfrac25$. After rebuy (Alice $15$ of $40$): $P(\text{ruin})=1-\tfrac{15}{40}=\tfrac58$. So $P(\text{Alice loses})=\tfrac25\cdot\tfrac58=\tfrac14$, giving $P(\text{Bob loses})=1-\tfrac14=\tfrac34.$

hints: Maximize the year first; years 2000s fail because the month needs a leading 0 or 1 that clashes.
The latest distinct-digit year that admits a valid MM/DD is 1987 (1988,1989 repeat digits).
Remaining digits {0,2,3,4,5,6}: max month 06, max day ≤30 from {2,3,4,5} is 25 ⇒ 06/25/1987.
distinct-date-ii.solution: For a $20XY$ year the digits $2,0$ are used, leaving no way to write a valid month (which needs a leading $0$ or $1$), so we look in the $1900$s. The latest year with all-distinct digits that still allows a valid date is $1987$ ($1988,1989$ repeat). With $\{0,2,3,4,5,6\}$ left, the largest month is $06$ and then the largest day $\le30$ from $\{2,3,4,5\}$ is $25$. So the date is $06/25/1987$.

hints: Symmetric walk with absorbing barriers 0 and 10.
P(reach 10 first) = start/total.
discrete-walker.solution: For a symmetric walk absorbed at $0$ and $10$, the probability of hitting $10$ first from position $k$ is $k/10$. From $6$ that's $\tfrac{6}{10}=\tfrac35.$

hints: Between consecutive digits there are 8 gaps.
Each gap is one of: +, *, or nothing (concatenate).
valid-expressions.solution: There are $8$ gaps between the digits $1$–$9$, and each can hold $+$, $*$, or nothing (joining digits). So there are $3^8=6561$ expressions.

hints: Cancel 6! from 10!.
Count paths through (2,3) and through (4,6) separately, then subtract those through both.
2d-paths-iii.solution: Paths through $(2,3)$: $\binom52\binom74=350$. Through $(4,6)$: $\binom{10}{4}\binom22=210$. Through both: $\binom52\binom52\binom22=100$. By inclusion–exclusion, $350+210-100=460.$

hints: Count sequences containing '111' (three consecutive 1s) among 6⁵.
Let B_i be 1s at positions i,i+1,i+2 (i=1,2,3); inclusion–exclusion.
consecutive-1s.solution: Count length-$5$ die sequences with three consecutive $1$s. Let $B_i$ force $1$s at positions $i,i+1,i+2$: $|B_i|=6^2=36$. Overlaps: $|B_1\cap B_2|=|B_2\cap B_3|=6$, $|B_1\cap B_3|=1$, triple $=1$. Inclusion–exclusion gives $108-13+1=96$, so $P=\tfrac{96}{6^5}=\tfrac{96}{7776}=\tfrac1{81}.$

hints: A rectangle is chosen by 2 of 2024 horizontal lines and 2 of 2024 vertical lines; it misses the shaded full row iff its row-span avoids row 1012.
Avoiding pairs: both horizontal lines in {0..1011} or both in {1012..2023}: 2·C(1012,2).
square-shade.solution: Choosing a rectangle uses $2$ of $2024$ horizontal lines (and any verticals). It avoids the shaded row $1012$ iff both horizontal lines lie in $\{0,\dots,1011\}$ or both in $\{1012,\dots,2023\}$ ($1012$ lines each): $2\binom{1012}{2}$ ways out of $\binom{2024}{2}$. That ratio is $\dfrac{1012\cdot1011}{1012\cdot2023}=\dfrac{1011}{2023}$, so $P(\text{contains shaded})=1-\dfrac{1011}{2023}=\dfrac{1012}{2023}.$

hints: Use Γ(a+1)=aΓ(a) to step Γ(9/4) down toward Γ(1/4).
The Z statistic uses the known population variance and a normal sampling distribution of the mean.
Conditions 1 (variance known), 2 (random sample), 3 (mean ~ normal) are required; n≥30 is not strictly necessary if normality holds.
Answer: 123.
statistical-test-review-v.solution: The $Z$ statistic $\dfrac{\bar X-\mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt n}$ requires a known population variance (1), a random sample (2), and an approximately normal sampling distribution of the mean (3). A size of at least $30$ (4) is not strictly required when normality already holds. So the answer is $123.$

hints: Count ordered triples (1–6 each) summing to 10.
There are 27 such ordered outcomes.
10-die-sum.solution: The number of ordered triples of dice summing to $10$ is $27$ (e.g. from the unordered partitions $(1,3,6),(1,4,5),(2,2,6),(2,3,5),(2,4,4),(3,3,4)$, weighted by their arrangements). So $P=\tfrac{27}{216}=\tfrac18.$

hints: The first flip sets the target side; the next three must match.
Each match has probability 1/2.
same-side.solution: The first flip is free; the remaining three must match it, each with probability $\tfrac12$. So $P=(\tfrac12)^3=\tfrac18.$

hints: Faces are 2,3,5,7,11,13; a prime sum >2 must be odd, so exactly one die shows the only even face, 2.
Then need 2+p prime: works for p=3,5,11 (sum 5,7,13) but not 7,13.
Favorable ordered outcomes: 2·3=6 of 36 ⇒ 1/6.
prime-pair.solution: The faces are $\{2,3,5,7,11,13\}$. A prime sum greater than $2$ is odd, so exactly one die must show the only even face $2$, and then $2+p$ must be prime: this holds for $p=3,5,11$ (giving $5,7,13$) but not $7,13$. That's $2\times3=6$ ordered outcomes of $36$, so $P=\tfrac16.$

hints: Only the 8 'key' cards (4 kings, 4 aces) matter; the m-th key card sits at expected deck position m·53/9.
Stop index T among keys: if the first key is a king, T=2; if it's an ace, T = index of the first king.
carded-pair.solution: Among the $8$ key cards ($4$K, $4$A), the $m$-th key lands at expected deck position $m\cdot\tfrac{53}{9}$ (the $8$ keys split $44$ others into $9$ gaps). Let $T$ be the stopping index among keys: if the first key is a king ($\tfrac12$), the next key ends it ($T=2$); if it's an ace ($\tfrac12$), we stop at the first king, whose index is $1+\tfrac{8}{5}=\tfrac{13}{5}$. So $\mathbb{E}[T]=\tfrac12\cdot2+\tfrac12\cdot\tfrac{13}{5}=\tfrac{23}{10}$, and $\mathbb{E}[\text{cards}]=\tfrac{53}{9}\cdot\tfrac{23}{10}=\tfrac{1219}{90}.$

hints: Conditioning on the whole sequence being even changes the distribution (don't just say Geom(1/3)).
P(N=n, all even)=(2/6)^{n−1}(1/6); P(all even)=1/4.
mossels-dice-problem.solution: Let $N$ be the roll of the first $6$. For 'all rolls even', the first $n-1$ must be in $\{2,4\}$: $P(N=n,\text{even})=(\tfrac26)^{n-1}\tfrac16$, and $P(\text{all even})=\tfrac{1/6}{1-1/3}=\tfrac14$. Then $\mathbb{E}[N\mid\text{even}]=\dfrac{\sum_n n(\tfrac13)^{n-1}\tfrac16}{1/4}=\tfrac23\cdot\dfrac1{(1-1/3)^2}=\tfrac32.$

hints: Let t(n) be tilings of 3×n; set up a coupled recursion with the 'bumpy' shape s(n).
threeway-tile.solution: With the standard coupled recursion for $3\times n$ domino tilings, $t(n)=4\,t(n-2)-t(n-4)$ with $t(0)=1,t(2)=3$. This yields $3,11,41,153$, so $t(8)=153.$

hints: Annie wins on turn 1 with prob 1/3, or both miss and the game resets.
Solve: p = 5/9.
With ρ=−1, the portfolio sd is |w₁σ₁ − w₂σ₂|; set it to 0.
w₁σ₁ = (1−w₁)σ₂ with σ₁=√0.15, σ₂=0.2.
the-perfect-hedge-i-.solution: For $\rho=-1$ the portfolio standard deviation is $|w_1\sigma_1-w_2\sigma_2|$, zero when $w_1\sigma_1=(1-w_1)\sigma_2$. With $\sigma_1=\sqrt{0.15}\approx0.387$ and $\sigma_2=\sqrt{0.04}=0.2$, $w_1=\dfrac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1+\sigma_2}=\dfrac{0.2}{0.587}\approx0.34.$

hints: Choose 5 of 21 blocks; count selections using exactly 2 colors.
Pick the 2 colors (C(3,2)=3); from their 14 blocks choose 5 using both: C(14,5)−2C(7,5).
building-blocks.solution: There are $\binom{21}{5}=20349$ equally likely selections. For exactly two colors: choose the pair ($\binom32=3$) and pick $5$ of their $14$ blocks using both colors, $\binom{14}5-2\binom75=2002-42=1960$. So favorable $=3\cdot1960=5880$ and $P=\dfrac{5880}{20349}.$

hints: Condition needs the first two and last two pairs to have the same colour multiset: GG/GG, YY/YY, or {G,Y}/{G,Y}.
jellybean-jar-ii.solution: The first two and last two must share the same colour multiset. Counting ordered draws from $6$G,$10$Y: all green $6{\cdot}5{\cdot}4{\cdot}3=360$; all yellow $5040$; and the mixed case (each pair one G one Y) $4\cdot(6{\cdot}5)(10{\cdot}9)=10800$. Total $16200$ over $16{\cdot}15{\cdot}14{\cdot}13=43680$, giving $\tfrac{135}{364}.$

hints: The coin (radius 1/2) stays inside a 2×2 square iff its center is ≥1/2 from each edge.
That center region is a 1×1 square inside the 2×2.
coin-on-chess-board.solution: The coin lies entirely within a square iff its center is at least $\tfrac12$ inch from every edge, i.e. inside a concentric $1\times1$ region of the $2\times2$ square. So $P=\dfrac{1^2}{2^2}=\dfrac14.$

hints: The 14 boys create 14 gaps; the girls fill 10 of them (C(14,10) ways).
Charlie stays between two boys iff both of his adjacent gaps are empty.
circular-charlie.solution: The girls occupy $10$ of the $14$ gaps between boys, $\binom{14}{10}$ ways. Charlie remains between two boys iff his two adjacent gaps are both empty, i.e. all $10$ girls go in the other $12$ gaps: $\dfrac{\binom{12}{10}}{\binom{14}{10}}=\dfrac{66}{1001}=\dfrac{6}{91}.$

hints: The median of 3 is < 2/3 iff at least 2 of the 3 values are < 2/3.
Each value < 2/3 with probability 2/3.
a-low-median.solution: The median of three is below $\tfrac23$ iff at least two values are below $\tfrac23$ (each with probability $\tfrac23$): $\binom32(\tfrac23)^2(\tfrac13)+(\tfrac23)^3=\tfrac{12}{27}+\tfrac{8}{27}=\tfrac{20}{27}.$

hints: This is the Josephus problem with step 2; person 1 always wins when N is a power of 2.
Writing N=X+L with X the largest power of 2 ≤N, the survivor is 2L+1=2N−2X+1.
clockwise-murder.solution: In the Josephus problem (every second person killed), if $N=2^k$ then person $1$ survives. Writing $N=X+L$ with $X=2^{\lfloor\log_2 N\rfloor}$, the survivor is $2L+1=2(N-X)+1=2N-2X+1$. Matching $aN+bX+c$: $a=2,b=-2,c=1$, so $a+2b+3c=2-4+3=1.$

hints: Cycle length = 40+4+40 = 84 s with 3 switch instants.
A 4-s interval catches a switch if its start falls in the 4 s before a switch: three length-4 windows.
Total favorable 12 s of 84 ⇒ 12/84 = 1/7.
light-switch.solution: The cycle is $40+4+40=84$ seconds with three switch instants. A $4$-second interval contains a switch iff its start lies in the $4$ seconds before one of them; these three length-$4$ windows are disjoint, totaling $12$ seconds. So $P=\dfrac{12}{84}=\dfrac17.$

hints: Only the 2♥ and the 12 face cards (K,Q,J) matter — 13 cards.
The condition is that the 2♥ comes before all 12 face cards.
By symmetry the 2♥ is first among these 13 with probability 1/13.
specific-card-pull-i.solution: The only relevant cards are the $2\heartsuit$ and the $12$ face cards. We need the $2\heartsuit$ to precede all of them, i.e. be first among these $13$ equally likely orderings: probability $\tfrac1{13}.$

hints: Rewrite max(3S,15)=15+3·max(S−5,0).
That's 15 bonds plus 3 calls at K=5.
price-an-option-ii.solution: $\max(3S_T,15)=15+3\max(S_T-5,0)$, replicated by $15$ bonds and $3$ calls struck at $5$. Its price is $15(0.9)+3(4.2)=13.5+12.6=26.1.$

hints: Pick the pair's rank & suits, then 3 distinct other ranks each with one suit.
poker-hands-iv.solution: Choose the pair's rank ($13$) and $2$ suits ($\binom42=6$); then $3$ distinct other ranks ($\binom{12}3$) each with one of $4$ suits. So $P=\dfrac{13\cdot6\cdot\binom{12}3\cdot4^3}{\binom{52}5}=\dfrac{1098240}{2598960}=\dfrac{352}{833}.$

hints: Match P(value=v) for one roll with P(sum=v) for two rolls, for v=2..6.
This forces p_2=p_1², p_3=2p_1³, p_4=5p_1⁴, p_5=14p_1⁵, p_6=42p_1⁶ (Catalan numbers).
Normalization Σp=1 gives coefficients 1,1,2,5,14,42 summing to 65.
single-double-sum.solution: Equating one-roll and two-roll probabilities for $2,\dots,6$ gives $p_2=p_1^2$, $p_3=2p_1^3$, $p_4=5p_1^4$, $p_5=14p_1^5$, $p_6=42p_1^6$ — the Catalan numbers. The normalization $\sum p_i=1$ reads $\sum_{k=1}^6 c_k p_1^k=1$ with $(c_k)=(1,1,2,5,14,42)$, whose sum is $65.$

hints: If x is the age at death, birth year = 29x and death year = 30x.
Need death year <1945 and birth year in the 1800s: x=64 ⇒ born 1856, died 1920.
His 1900 birthday age = 1900−1856 = 44.
1900-age.solution: If $x$ is his age at death, the birth year is $29x$ and the death year $30x$. Requiring a death before $1945$ and a 19th-century birth gives $x=64$: born $1856$, died $1920$. On his $1900$ birthday he turned $1900-1856=44.$

hints: Perimeter P = AC+AB+BC; differentiate.
= −1 units/sec (lengths irrelevant).
triangular-change.solution: The perimeter's rate is the sum of the side rates: $\dfrac{dP}{dt}=3+(-2)+(-2)=-1$ units per second (the current lengths don't matter).

hints: This is symmetric; both reroll below a threshold k, aiming to win not to maximize value.
Indifference at the threshold yields k²+k−1=0.
competitive-sampling.solution: By symmetry both players reroll when below a threshold $k$. The optimal $k$ is where you're indifferent between keeping $k$ and rerolling against the opponent's same strategy, which reduces to $k^2+k-1=0$. The positive root is $k=\dfrac{\sqrt5-1}{2}\approx0.618.$

hints: Each roll is 'prime' (2,3,5) with probability 1/2.
With equal rolls each would lead with probability 1/2; the extra roll breaks ties symmetrically.
P(player 2 leads) = 1/2.
too-many-primes-to-count.solution: Each roll is prime with probability $\tfrac12$. With the same number of rolls, by symmetry each player leads with equal probability and ties split evenly; giving player 2 one extra roll exactly balances this, so $P(\text{player 2 has more})=\tfrac12.$

hints: Each element is in A∩B with probability 1/4 (independently).
overlapping-subsets.solution: Each of the $20$ elements is in $A\cap B$ independently with probability $\tfrac12\cdot\tfrac12=\tfrac14$, so $N\sim\mathrm{Binom}(20,\tfrac14)$ with $\mathbb{E}[N]=5$ and $\mathrm{Var}(N)=20\cdot\tfrac14\cdot\tfrac34=\tfrac{15}4$. Thus $\dfrac{\mathrm{Var}(N)}{\mathbb{E}[N]}=\dfrac{15/4}{5}=\dfrac34.$

hints: Bob's gain picking x: lose Y if x>Y, gain x if x<Y.
Maximize: x=33 or 34 give 3366/200 = 16.83.
big-smalls.solution: If Bob picks $x$, he loses $Y$ when $x>Y$ and gains $x$ when $x<Y$: $\mathbb{E}[Z_x]=-\dfrac{(x-1)x}{200}+\dfrac{x(100-x)}{100}=\dfrac{x(201-3x)}{200}$. Maximizing over integers gives $x=33$ or $34$, both yielding $\dfrac{3366}{200}=16.83.$

hints: There are C(12,4) candidate 4-circuits; use an indicator for each.
A 4-circuit needs all C(4,2)=6 wires active: probability (1/2)⁶.
combinatorial-electrician.solution: There are $\binom{12}{4}=495$ sets of $4$ bulbs, each forming a complete circuit iff all $\binom42=6$ connecting wires are active, probability $(\tfrac12)^6=\tfrac1{64}$. By linearity, $\mathbb{E}=\dfrac{495}{64}.$

hints: WLOG cards are 1,2,3; the strategy can only use the difference of the first two cards (n is unknown).
After flipping: on difference +2 keep (it's the max); on a −1 difference take the third; otherwise stay/decide to maximize.
Averaging the six orderings gives 14/6=7/3=n+4/3, so c=4/3.
unknown-starter.solution: Take the cards as $1,2,3$ (shift by $n-1$). Since $n$ is unknown, decisions can only use the observed difference of the first two cards. Flipping the first card (staying yields only $2$), then for each ordered pair choosing optimally on the difference: outcomes are $2,3,3,1,3,2$ for $(1,2),(2,3),(2,1),(3,2),(1,3),(3,1)$, averaging $\tfrac{14}{6}=\tfrac73$. So the expected payout is $n+\tfrac43$, giving $c=\tfrac43.$

hints: E[(A₁−Ā)²]=Var(A₁−Ā)+(E[A₁−Ā])².
mean-difference.solution: $\mathbb{E}[(A_1-\bar A)^2]=\mathrm{Var}(A_1-\bar A)+(\mathbb{E}[A_1-\bar A])^2$. Here $\mathbb{E}[A_1]=1$, $\bar\mu=\tfrac1{10}\sum(2i-1)=10$, so the mean term is $(1-10)^2=81$. Writing $A_1-\bar A=\tfrac9{10}A_1-\tfrac1{10}\sum_{i=2}^{10}A_i$ with independent variance-$100$ terms: $\mathrm{Var}=\tfrac{81}{100}(100)+\tfrac{9}{100}(100)=90$. Total $=90+81=171.$

hints: You have 7- and 11-pint vessels and want exactly 2 pints, minimizing fill/empty/pour transactions.
Since gcd(7,11)=1, 2 is reachable; repeatedly pour 7 into 11 and reset to step the remainder.
Track states (a,b) and count moves to first reach a 2 in some vessel.

cheese-lover-i.solution: Each block has mean $250$, so $\mathbb{E}[T_{100}]=25000$. By Markov's inequality, $P(T_{100}>26000)\le\dfrac{25000}{26000}=\dfrac{25}{26}.$

hints: Stop when the running sum is even; condition on the first roll's parity.
Even first (1/2): done in 1. Odd first (1/2): need a later odd to flip parity, Geom(1/2) more rolls (mean 2).
die-multiple-i.solution: If the first roll is even (prob $\tfrac12$) the sum is even and we stop. If odd, we need a subsequent odd roll to make the sum even, which takes a further $\mathrm{Geom}(\tfrac12)$ rolls (mean $2$). So $E=\tfrac12(1)+\tfrac12(1+2)=2.$

hints: Lap rates: 1/6 and 1/9 laps per minute.
Lapping = gaining a full lap at the relative rate 1/6 − 1/9 = 1/18.
Time = 1/(1/18) = 18 minutes.
lapping.solution: Lauda gains on Hunt at $\tfrac16-\tfrac19=\tfrac1{18}$ lap per minute. To gain a full lap takes $\dfrac{1}{1/18}=18$ minutes.

hints: This is a growing perpetuity: value = CF/(r − g).
Maximize: derivative 500/(n+1)² − 5 = 0 ⇒ (n+1)²=100 ⇒ n=9.
optimal-bidders-i.solution: With $n$ bidders, $\mathbb{E}[\max]=500+\tfrac{500n}{n+1}$, so the payout is $f(n)=1000-\tfrac{500}{n+1}-5n$. Setting $f'(n)=\tfrac{500}{(n+1)^2}-5=0$ gives $n+1=10$, $n=9$, and $f(9)=1000-50-45=905.$

hints: For a Poisson process, P(none in 10 min)=P(none in hour)^{1/6}=0.8.
stranded-at-sea.solution: $P(\text{no boat in an hour})=1-0.737856=0.262144=0.8^6$. Over $10$ minutes ($\tfrac16$ hour) the no-boat probability is $0.8^{6\cdot(1/6)}=0.8$, so $P(\ge1\text{ boat in 10 min})=1-0.8=0.2.$

hints: Y=I·X with I~Bernoulli(1/2) (heads) and X independent (mean 12, var 2).
greedy-bob.solution: $Y=IX$ with $I\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(\tfrac12)$ independent of $X$ ($\mathbb{E}[X]=12$, $\mathrm{Var}(X)=2$). Then $\mathbb{E}[Y]=\tfrac12\cdot12=6$ and $\mathbb{E}[Y^2]=\tfrac12\,\mathbb{E}[X^2]=\tfrac12(2+144)=73$, so $\mathrm{Var}(Y)=73-36=37.$

hints: Total money is always 8.96. The i-th flipper, just after paying out, holds 1.28/2^{7−i}; before flipping he holds 4.48+0.64/2^{7−i}.
Earlier heads double his initial, so x_i=(4.48+0.64/2^{7−i})/2^{i−1}.
stack-double.solution: The pot is always $\$8.96$. Player $i$ (the $i$-th to flip), right after paying everyone, holds $\tfrac{1.28}{2^{7-i}}$, so before flipping he holds $S_i=4.48+\tfrac{0.64}{2^{7-i}}$. Since the earlier $i-1$ heads doubled his initial stake, $x_i=S_i/2^{i-1}$, giving $x=(4.49,2.25,1.13,0.57,0.29,0.15,0.08)$. Then $\sum x_i^2=26.9374$, so $10000\sum x_i^2=269374.$

hints: Count digit 9 over 000–999 (1000 numbers, 3 positions).
Each position shows 9 in 1/10 of numbers: 100 times per position.
9-appearance.solution: Writing numbers $000$–$999$, each of the $3$ digit positions is a $9$ in exactly $\tfrac1{10}$ of the $1000$ numbers, i.e. $100$ times. Total $3\cdot100=300$, and $1000$ contributes no $9$s, so $300.$

hints: Alice wins iff A > max(B₁,B₂); ties go to Bob. P(max<a)=[P(B<a)]².
Sum (1/30)Σ_{a=1}^{30}[P(B<a)]², with P(B<a)=(a−1)/20 for a≤21 and 1 beyond.
2030-split-ii.solution: Alice wins iff her roll beats both of Bob's, $A>\max(B_1,B_2)$, with $P(\max<a)=\big(P(B<a)\big)^2$. Then $P(W)=\tfrac1{30}\sum_{a=1}^{30}\big(P(B<a)\big)^2$ where $P(B<a)=\tfrac{a-1}{20}$ for $a\le21$ and $1$ for $a>20$. The sum is $\tfrac1{400}\sum_{k=0}^{19}k^2+10=6.175+10=\tfrac{647}{40}$, so $P(W)=\tfrac1{30}\cdot\tfrac{647}{40}=\tfrac{647}{1200}.$

hints: Condition on U=u: each X_n exceeds u with probability 1−u.
N|U=u ~ Geom(1−u), so E[N|U=u]=1/(1−u).
E[N]=∫₀¹ 1/(1−u) du diverges ⇒ infinite (−1).
uniform-fix.solution: Given $U=u$, each $X_n>u$ with probability $1-u$, so $N\mid U=u\sim\mathrm{Geom}(1-u)$ with mean $\tfrac1{1-u}$. Then $\mathbb{E}[N]=\int_0^1\tfrac1{1-u}\,du=\infty$, so the answer is $-1.$

hints: The unit operation ferries one soldier across and returns both children to the start.
That cycle is 4 crossings: 2 kids over, 1 back, 1 soldier over, 1 kid back.
358 soldiers × 4 = 1432 crossings (children end with the boat).
crossing-the-river.solution: To move one soldier across while keeping both children available takes $4$ crossings: both children row over, one returns, a soldier crosses, the other child returns. Repeating for all $358$ soldiers gives $358\times4=1432$ crossings, leaving the two children with the boat.

hints: You miss the location only if neither spin reveals it: (2/5)²=4/25.
Otherwise you know it; if not, you guess 1/3.
missing-million-ii.solution: The wheel fails both times with probability $(\tfrac25)^2=\tfrac4{25}$. So $P(\text{locate})=\big(1-\tfrac4{25}\big)+\tfrac4{25}\cdot\tfrac13=\tfrac{63}{75}+\tfrac4{75}=\tfrac{67}{75}.$

hints: Total ways to pick 5 of 8 minus those with both enemies.
C(8,5)=56; both enemies in ⇒ choose 3 of remaining 6 = C(6,3)=20.
unfriendly.solution: Total invitations: $\binom85=56$. Those including both enemies: $\binom63=20$. So $56-20=36.$

hints: Track tails t; each round flips a pair of tails (2 flips), t→t−2,t−1,t with prob 1/4,1/2,1/4.
Add the 4 initial flips and average over t~Binom(4,½): total = 176/27.
coin-pair-iv.solution: Let $E_t$ be the expected additional flips with $t$ tails. Flipping a pair: $E_t=2+\tfrac14E_{t-2}+\tfrac12E_{t-1}+\tfrac14E_t$, giving $E_2=\tfrac83,E_3=\tfrac{40}9,E_4=\tfrac{176}{27}$. The initial $4$ flips plus the average over $t\sim\mathrm{Binom}(4,\tfrac12)$ is $4+\tfrac{68}{27}=\tfrac{176}{27}.$

hints: Cube types: corner (8, 3 red faces), edge (12, 2), face (6, 1), center (1, 0). P(two reds)=(red/6)².
P(2 red): corner 1/4, edge 1/9, face 1/36, center 0.
better-in-red-v.solution: By type, $P(\text{two reds})$ is $\tfrac14$ (corner), $\tfrac19$ (edge), $\tfrac1{36}$ (face), $0$ (center), with priors $\tfrac{8,12,6,1}{27}$. Total $=\tfrac1{27}(8\cdot\tfrac14+12\cdot\tfrac19+6\cdot\tfrac1{36})=\tfrac{7}{54}$, and corner contributes $\tfrac{8}{27}\cdot\tfrac14=\tfrac2{27}$. So $P(\text{corner}\mid\text{2 red})=\dfrac{2/27}{7/54}=\dfrac47.$

hints: You want the largest coin total with no subset summing to exactly 100¢.
Avoid being able to reach $1: 3 quarters + 4 dimes + 4 pennies.
dollar-break.solution: With $3$ quarters ($75$), $4$ dimes ($40$), and $4$ pennies ($4$) you hold $\$1.19$, yet no subset sums to exactly $100$ cents (a $4$th quarter or proper dime combination is unavailable). Any more coins would enable change, so the maximum is $\$1.19.$

hints: A sub-box is fixed by choosing 2 of the 6 grid planes along each axis.
Count = [C(6,2)]³ for a 5×5×5.
hidden-prisms.solution: A rectangular prism is determined by choosing $2$ of the $6$ parallel cutting planes along each of the three axes: $\binom62=15$ per axis. So the count is $15^3=3375.$

hints: For p<1/2 the particle reaches +1 with probability <1 (here 1/2).
With positive probability it never arrives.
So the expected number of steps is infinite ⇒ −1.
particle-reach-v.solution: For $p=\tfrac13<\tfrac12$, the walk reaches $+1$ only with probability $\tfrac12<1$; with the remaining probability it drifts to $-\infty$ and never arrives. Hence the expected number of steps is infinite, so the answer is $-1.$

hints: Find the risk-neutral up-probability from u=1.9, d=0.7.
Enumerate the 2-step terminal prices and which exceed 2.
Price = risk-neutral probability that S_T > 2 (discounted).
Each bowl gets 3 balls; let w_i be its whites (0–3), with Σw_i=7 (then blacks are forced).
Count solutions of w₁+…+w₅=7 with 0≤w_i≤3.
bowl-distributions.solution: Each bowl's white count $w_i\in\{0,1,2,3\}$ with $\sum w_i=7$ (blacks $3-w_i$ then sum to $8$). The number of capped solutions is $\binom{11}{4}-5\binom{7}{4}=330-175=155.$

hints: Total pairings of 10 gloves: 10!/(2⁵5!)=945. Count valid ones (partners differ by ≤1).
Condition on the two value-n gloves: pair them (f(n−1)) or match both to the two (n−1)'s (2·f(n−2)).
1-glove-off.solution: There are $\dfrac{10!}{2^5 5!}=945$ pairings. Let $f(n)$ count valid pairings of two copies each of $1..n$. The two $n$'s either pair together (leaving $f(n-1)$) or both pair with the two $(n-1)$'s in $2$ ways (leaving $f(n-2)$): $f(n)=f(n-1)+2f(n-2)$ with $f(1)=1,f(2)=3$. So $f(5)=21$ and $P=\tfrac{21}{945}=\tfrac1{45}.$

hints: P(M_n ≤ ln n)=P(all X_i ≤ ln n)=(1−e^{−ln n})ⁿ.
exponential-maximum-asymptotic.solution: $P(M_n\le\ln n)=\prod P(X_i\le\ln n)=(1-e^{-\ln n})^n=(1-\tfrac1n)^n\to e^{-1}=\tfrac1e$. So $b=1.$

hints: A triangle fails iff one side exceeds the sum of the other two; the three failure events are disjoint.
Each has probability 1/6 by symmetry (e.g. P(X>Y+Z)=1/6).
uniform-triangle.solution: No triangle forms iff one length exceeds the other two combined; these three events are disjoint and each has probability $\tfrac16$ (e.g. $P(X>Y+Z)=\tfrac16$). So $P(\text{triangle})=1-3\cdot\tfrac16=\tfrac12.$

hints: Write X=Z₁, Y=ρZ₁+√(1−ρ²)Z₂ with Z₁,Z₂ IID N(0,1).
Cov(X,Y²)=E[Z₁Y²]; expand Y² — all terms involve odd moments of Z₁ (or E[Z₂])=0.
Cov(X,Y²)=0.
bivariate-covariance.solution: With $X=Z_1$, $Y=\rho Z_1+\sqrt{1-\rho^2}Z_2$: $\mathbb{E}[XY^2]=\rho^2\mathbb{E}[Z_1^3]+2\rho\sqrt{1-\rho^2}\mathbb{E}[Z_1^2 Z_2]+(1-\rho^2)\mathbb{E}[Z_1 Z_2^2]=0$ (each term has $\mathbb{E}[Z_1^3]=0$ or $\mathbb{E}[Z_2]=0$). Since $\mathbb{E}[X]=0$, $\mathrm{Cov}(X,Y^2)=0.$

hints: By symmetry the answer is twice the expected tosses for Anna (3 heads) to win.
Anna wins in 3,4,5 tosses with probabilities 1/8, 3/16, 6/32.
For two IID uniforms on 1..n, E[|X−Y|]=(n²−1)/(3n).
Plug n=30.
30-side-difference.solution: For two independent uniform rolls on $1,\dots,n$, $\mathbb{E}[|X-Y|]=\dfrac{n^2-1}{3n}$. With $n=30$ this is $\dfrac{899}{90}.$

hints: Fix the red value; each blue matches it independently with probability 1/6.
Exactly one of two blues matches: C(2,1)(1/6)(5/6).
redblue-die-match.solution: Given the red value, each blue die matches it with probability $\tfrac16$. Exactly one of the two blues matching is $\binom21\tfrac16\cdot\tfrac56=\tfrac{10}{36}=\tfrac{5}{18}.$

hints: With n noodles (2n ends), each join closes a loop with probability 1/(2k−1) when 2k ends remain.
ramen-bowl.solution: When $2k$ ends remain, picking a first end, the chance its partner is its own other end (closing a loop) is $\tfrac1{2k-1}$. By linearity $\mathbb{E}[\text{circles}]=\sum_{k=1}^{100}\tfrac1{2k-1}\approx3.28$, which rounds to $3.$

hints: Underlying = product of two dice; call pays max(X−30,0).
Only X=36 (6×6) exceeds 30, paying 6.
option-dice-i.solution: The call on the product pays $\max(X-30,0)$, nonzero only when $X=36$ (the roll $6,6$), paying $6$. So the price is $6\cdot\tfrac1{36}=\tfrac16.$

hints: Count non-crossing perfect matchings of 6 points vs all matchings.
Non-crossing matchings = Catalan C₃ = 5; total matchings = 5!! = 15.
crossing-pairs.solution: The number of non-crossing perfect matchings of $6$ points on a circle is the Catalan number $C_3=5$, out of $5!!=15$ total matchings. So $P=\tfrac{5}{15}=\tfrac13.$

hints: After the first pass, the survivors are the even numbers; the pattern repeats on a halved set.
The last number deleted is the highest power of 2 within the range.
Largest power of 2 ≤ 2000 is 1024.
circular-delete.solution: Each pass deletes every other number, so after the first pass only multiples of $2$ remain, then multiples of $4$, and so on. The number surviving longest (last deleted) is the largest power of $2$ in range, $2^{10}=1024.$

hints: Use the hedge weights from ρ=−1: w₁=σ₂/(σ₁+σ₂)≈0.341, w₂≈0.659.
Portfolio return = 0.04w₁ + 0.02w₂.
the-perfect-hedge-ii.solution: The risk-free weights are $w_1=\dfrac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1+\sigma_2}\approx0.341$, $w_2\approx0.659$. The portfolio's expected return is $0.04(0.341)+0.02(0.659)\approx0.0268$, i.e. $2.68\%.$

hints: E[X]=1/2; compute E[X²]=∫₀¹x²dx=1/3.
The 12-sided die averages 6.5, so keep the d8 only if it beats 6.5 (a 7 or 8).
Keep 7,8 (prob 2/8); else reroll for 6.5.
Apply Itô to e^{κt}X_t; the drift becomes κθe^{κt}dt and the noise is a mean-zero Itô integral.
Taking expectations: E[X_T]=θ+(x−θ)e^{−κT}.
silly-sde.solution: By Itô, $d(e^{\kappa t}X_t)=e^{\kappa t}(\kappa\theta\,dt-\sigma\sqrt{X_t}\,dW_t)$. Taking expectations (the Itô integral has mean $0$): $e^{\kappa T}\mathbb{E}[X_T]-x=\theta(e^{\kappa T}-1)$, so $\mathbb{E}[X_T]=\theta+(x-\theta)e^{-\kappa T}$. With the given values, $2+3e^{-2}$, giving $A=2,B=3,C=-2$ and $ABC=-12.$

hints: For A−B: your bid = bid_A − ask_B, your ask = ask_A − bid_B.
The final length is a run of evens ending in a 1, conditioned on no 3 or 5 occurring in that run.
P(length=k, success)=(1/2)^{k−1}(1/6); P(success)=1/3.
sequence-terminator.solution: The final sequence is the successful run: evens then a terminating $1$, with no $3$/$5$. $P(\text{length}=k,\text{success})=(\tfrac12)^{k-1}\tfrac16$ and $P(\text{success})=\tfrac13$. So $\mathbb{E}[\text{length}\mid\text{success}]=\dfrac{\tfrac16\sum_k k(\tfrac12)^{k-1}}{1/3}=\dfrac{(1/6)\cdot4}{1/3}=2.$

hints: Digits(m)=⌊log₁₀ m⌋+1. Find digit counts of 2^1000 and 5^1000.
number-concatenate.solution: The digit count is $\lfloor\log_{10}m\rfloor+1$: $2^{1000}$ has $\lfloor1000\log_{10}2\rfloor+1=302$ digits and $5^{1000}$ has $699$. Concatenated, $n$ has $302+699=1001$ digits (matching $2^{1000}5^{1000}=10^{1000}$).

hints: This is a Pólya urn: starting 1 made, 1 missed, the success rule makes the made-count uniform.
The final number of goals is uniform over its 99 possible values (1 to 99).
P(exactly 66)=1/99.
soccer-practice.solution: Starting with one make and one miss, the rule 'score with probability = current fraction made' is a Pólya urn, under which the final number of goals is uniformly distributed over its possible values $1,\dots,99$. Hence $P(\text{exactly }66)=\tfrac1{99}.$

hints: Track expected final tails h_t; h_t=¼h_{t−2}+½h_{t−1}+¼h_t with h_0=0,h_1=1.
h_2=2/3, h_3=7/9, h_4=20/27; average over t~Binom(4,½): E[final tails]=20/27.
coin-pair-iii.solution: Let $h_t$ be expected final tails from $t$ tails: $h_t=\tfrac14h_{t-2}+\tfrac12h_{t-1}+\tfrac14h_t$, giving $h_2=\tfrac23,h_3=\tfrac79,h_4=\tfrac{20}{27}$. Averaging over $t\sim\mathrm{Binom}(4,\tfrac12)$ gives $\mathbb{E}[\text{final tails}]=\tfrac{20}{27}$, so $\mathbb{E}[\text{heads}]=4-\tfrac{20}{27}=\tfrac{88}{27}.$

hints: After removing a pair: one rank has 2 cards, nine ranks have 4 each (38 total).
Favorable pairs: 1 (depleted rank) + 9·C(4,2)=54.
modified-deck-pair.solution: After removing a matched pair, the deck has $38$ cards: one rank with $2$, nine ranks with $4$. The number of matching-pair draws is $\binom22+9\binom42=1+54=55$ out of $\binom{38}{2}=703$, so $P=\tfrac{55}{703}.$

hints: Multiple of 15 ⇒ divisible by 5 (ends in 0) and by 3 (digit sum divisible by 3).
Digits are 0/1, so the digit sum (number of 1s) must be a multiple of 3 — at least three 1s.
Smallest: 1110.
least-multiple-of-15.solution: It must end in $0$ (divisible by $5$) and have digit sum divisible by $3$; with only $1$s and $0$s that means at least three $1$s. The smallest such number is $1110.$

hints: Count solutions of X₁+X₂+X₃=N with each in {0,…,N}: C(N+2,2) (caps don't bind).
precise-n-sum.solution: The number of ways to write $X_1+X_2+X_3=N$ with each in $\{0,\dots,N\}$ is $\binom{N+2}{2}$, so $p_N=\dfrac{\binom{N+2}{2}}{(N+1)^3}=\dfrac{N+2}{2(N+1)^2}$. Then $Np_N\to\dfrac12.$

hints: Arrangements of a multiset = 19!/(3!·7!·9!).
Compute the multinomial coefficient.
colorful-line.solution: The number of distinct arrangements is the multinomial $\dfrac{19!}{3!\,7!\,9!}=11085360.$

hints: Σ over compositions of n into k parts of the multinomial = k^n (assign each of n items to one of k bins).
Here k=4, n=7.
multinomial-sum.solution: Summing all multinomials $\binom{7}{b_1,b_2,b_3,b_4}$ counts all ways to place $7$ distinct items into $4$ boxes, which is $4^7=16384.$

hints: With a max of N drops, the first egg's drops cover N + (N−1) + … floors.
Smallest such N is 14.
X,Y are multinomial counts; Cov(X,Y)=−n p₁p₅, Var(X)=n p(1−p).
Cov=−n/36, Var=5n/36.
upface-correlation.solution: For multinomial counts, $\mathrm{Cov}(X,Y)=-n p_1 p_5=-\tfrac{n}{36}$ and $\mathrm{Var}(X)=\mathrm{Var}(Y)=n\cdot\tfrac16\cdot\tfrac56=\tfrac{5n}{36}$. So $\mathrm{Corr}(X,Y)=\dfrac{-n/36}{5n/36}=-\dfrac15.$

hints: P(exit on a vertical side) is harmonic: average of neighbours, =1 on x=0,4 and 0 on y=0,4.
Use the symmetries x↔4−x and y↔4−y to reduce to a few unknowns.
Solving gives the center =1/2 and u(1,2)=5/8.
square-billy.solution: Let $u(x,y)=P(\text{exit on a vertical side})$, harmonic with $u=1$ on $x\in\{0,4\}$ and $0$ on $y\in\{0,4\}$. Using the reflection symmetries, the interior unknowns reduce to a small system whose solution gives $u(2,2)=\tfrac12$ and $u(1,2)=\tfrac58.$

hints: The combined experiment has 6·6·2 equally likely outcomes.
The smallest nonzero probability is a single outcome.
smallest-probability.solution: Two die rolls and a coin flip give $6\cdot6\cdot2=72$ equally likely outcomes, so the smallest simulable nonzero probability is $\tfrac1{72}.$

hints: #1 always reaches the final; #3 reaches it iff #1 and #2 are both in the other half.
P(#3 in the half opposite #1)=64/127; then P(#2 also in #1's half)=63/126=1/2.
chess-tournament-ii.solution: #1 always reaches the final. For #3 to reach it, #3 must be the strongest in its half — i.e. #1 and #2 are both in the other half. $P(\#3\text{ opposite }\#1)=\tfrac{64}{127}$, and then $P(\#2\text{ in }\#1\text{'s half})=\tfrac{63}{126}=\tfrac12$. So $P=\tfrac{64}{127}\cdot\tfrac12=\tfrac{32}{127}.$

hints: Submartingale ⟺ E[((1/2)e^{(λ/2)X})^p] ≥ 1, with X~Exp(λ).
This equals 2^{1−p}/(2−p) ≥ 1, i.e. 2^{1−p} ≥ 2−p (and p<2 for convergence).
Holds for p∈[1,2): a=1, b=2, a+b=3.
make-your-martingale-iii.solution: $M_n^p$ is a submartingale iff $\mathbb{E}\big[(\tfrac12 e^{\frac{\lambda}2 X})^p\big]\ge1$. With $X\sim\mathrm{Exp}(\lambda)$, this is $(\tfrac12)^p\cdot\dfrac{2}{2-p}=\dfrac{2^{1-p}}{2-p}\ge1$, i.e. $2^{1-p}\ge2-p$, which holds for $p\in(1,2)$ (needing $p<2$ to converge). So $a=1,b=2$ and $a+b=3.$

hints: Count independent sets of the 3×3 grid graph (no two on-bulbs adjacent).
There are 63 such configurations.
power-grid.solution: The valid patterns are exactly the independent sets of the $3\times3$ grid graph, of which there are $63$. Each of the $2^9$ configurations is equally likely, so $P=\dfrac{63}{512}.$

hints: Each breaking order is an interleaving of the 4 columns' forced bottom-up sequences.
lowest-target.solution: Within a column the order is forced, so a full breaking sequence is an interleaving of the columns: $\dfrac{12!}{2!\,3!\,3!\,4!}=277200.$

hints: The circle stays inside iff the center is ≥r from each side: a (20−2r)² region.
P(inside | R=r)=(20−2r)²/400; average over r~U(0,10).
square-cross.solution: Given radius $r$, the circle avoids the square's sides iff its center lies in the inner $(20-2r)^2$ region, probability $\tfrac{(20-2r)^2}{400}$. Averaging over $R\sim U(0,10)$: $\tfrac1{10}\int_0^{10}\tfrac{(20-2r)^2}{400}dr=\tfrac1{4000}\cdot\tfrac{4000}{3}=\tfrac13.$

hints: Call prices must be convex in strike; check C(1000)−2C(1010)+C(1020).
= 4 − 7 + 2.75 = −0.25 < 0, so a long butterfly costs −0.25 (a credit) with payoff ≥ 0.
Guaranteed profit = 0.25.
option-arbitrage.solution: Call prices should be convex in strike, but $C(1000)-2C(1010)+C(1020)=4-7+2.75=-0.25<0$. Buying that butterfly (long the $1000$ and $1020$, short two $1010$s) gives a $0.25$ credit with a payoff that is always $\ge0$, so you are guaranteed $\$0.25.$

hints: A pair remains iff both its socks are among the 4 left; use an indicator per pair.
P(both of a pair among the remaining 4)=C(10,2)/C(12,4)=1/11.
matching-socks-ii.solution: After drawing $8$, four socks remain. A given pair survives iff both its socks are among those $4$: $\dfrac{\binom{10}{2}}{\binom{12}{4}}=\dfrac{45}{495}=\dfrac1{11}$. By linearity over $6$ pairs, $\mathbb{E}=\dfrac{6}{11}.$

hints: Match = both blue or both red; jars independent.
Keep H heads if H≥2; reflip (net value 2−1=1) if H≤1.
E=Σ_{H≥2}H·P(H) + P(H≤1)·1, with H~Binom(4,½).
flip-again.solution: With $H\sim\mathrm{Binom}(4,\tfrac12)$: keep $H$ if $H\ge2$, else reflip for net $2-1=1$. So $\mathbb{E}=\big(2\cdot\tfrac6{16}+3\cdot\tfrac4{16}+4\cdot\tfrac1{16}\big)+\tfrac5{16}\cdot1=\tfrac{28}{16}+\tfrac5{16}=\tfrac{33}{16}.$

hints: Y=n−X, so E[XY]=nE[X]−E[X²] with X~Binom(n,½).
Only the prime 2 is even; a sum of four primes is even iff 2 is NOT among them (then all four are odd).
P(2 not chosen)=C(15,4)/C(16,4).
picky-primes.solution: Among the first $16$ primes only $2$ is even. The sum of four chosen primes is even iff $2$ is excluded (leaving four odds). So $P=\dfrac{\binom{15}{4}}{\binom{16}{4}}=\dfrac{12}{16}=\dfrac34.$

hints: Use the Fibonacci generating function Σ F_k x^k = x/(1−x−x²).
The sum is (1/10)·ΣF_k(1/10)^k with x=1/10.
Var(R_A+kR_B)=Var_A+k²Var_B+2k·Cov, with Cov=ρσ_Aσ_B.
= 4+9k²−6k; minimize over k.
minimum-variance-portfolio.solution: With $\mathrm{Cov}=(-\tfrac12)(2)(3)=-3$, the portfolio variance is $4+9k^2-6k$. Setting the derivative $18k-6=0$ gives $k=\tfrac13$ shares of $B$.

hints: Imperfections in 10 sq yd ~ Poisson(40); cost = 10·N.
poisson-review-v.solution: Imperfections over $10$ square yards are $\mathrm{Poisson}(40)$, and cost $=10N$. So mean $=10\cdot40=400$ and variance $=10^2\cdot40=4000$, summing to $4400.$

hints: The put is out-of-the-money (S=35>K=33); long-option theta is negative and most negative near ATM.
Raising S moves it further OTM, so |theta| shrinks (theta becomes less negative).
Theta increases ⇒ 1.
put-theta.solution: The put is out-of-the-money and a long option's theta is negative, largest in magnitude near the money. Increasing $S$ pushes the put further OTM, shrinking $|\theta|$ so theta rises toward $0$: the answer is $1.$

hints: Arriving at a uniform time, the wait to the next bus is Uniform(0,10).
Its mean is the midpoint.
= 5 minutes.
bus-wait-i.solution: A uniformly random arrival makes the wait $\mathrm{Unif}(0,10)$, with mean $\tfrac{0+10}{2}=5$ minutes.

hints: With σ=0 the SDE is the ODE dS/S = μ dt.
The top three sum to 18 only if all three are 6s, i.e. at least three 6s among the 5 dice.
the-big-three.solution: A top-three sum of $18$ requires three $6$s, i.e. at least three $6$s among the five dice: $\sum_{k=3}^{5}\binom5k(\tfrac16)^k(\tfrac56)^{5-k}=\dfrac{250+25+1}{7776}=\dfrac{276}{7776}=\dfrac{23}{648}.$

hints: Of 8 outcomes, 7 have at least one head.
Exactly one head: HTT, THT, TTH — 3 outcomes.
1-head-up.solution: Conditioning on at least one head leaves $7$ equally likely outcomes; exactly one head occurs in $3$ of them, so $P=\tfrac37.$

hints: Only 5s and 6s matter; before the first 6 they are equally likely.
The number of 5s before the first 6 is geometric with mean P(5)/P(6).
exact-5-ii.solution: Ignoring other faces, each relevant roll is a $5$ or $6$ equally likely. The number of $5$s before the first $6$ has mean $\dfrac{P(5)}{P(6)}=\dfrac{1/6}{1/6}=1.$

hints: Pair consecutive terms and use a²−b²=(a+b)(a−b).
Each pair (2k)²−(2k−1)² = 4k−1; or each n²−(n−1)²=2n−1.
Sum of all integers 1..100 = 5050.
alternating-sum.solution: Each pair $n^2-(n-1)^2=2n-1$, so the expression telescopes to $\sum_{n=1}^{100}n=\dfrac{100\cdot101}{2}=5050.$

hints: Complement: the point is in the top portion (height >1), itself a small cone.
Top cone has radius 1/2, height 1: volume (1/3)π(1/2)²·1 = π/12, vs full 2π/3.
(X,Y) is radially symmetric, so the angle is uniform.
Y>0 is the upper half (180°); Y>√3X within it is the arc from 60° to 180° (120°).
positively-normal.solution: By radial symmetry the direction angle is uniform. The event $Y>0$ is the upper half-plane ($180^\circ$), and within it $Y>\sqrt3X$ corresponds to angles from $60^\circ$ to $180^\circ$ ($120^\circ$). So the conditional probability is $\tfrac{120}{180}=\tfrac23.$

hints: X=e^Z, so Var(X⁴)=E[e^{8Z}]−(E[e^{4Z}])²; use the normal MGF E[e^{tZ}]=e^{t²/2}.
Var=e³²−e¹⁶ ⇒ a+b=48.
Alfred = 1.2·John and Alfred = 1.25·Charles.
sheep-sharing.solution: Alfred gets $1.2\times3600=4320$. Since Alfred is $25\%$ more than Charles, $\text{Charles}=\dfrac{4320}{1.25}=3456.$

hints: Distinct win counts must be 0,1,…,31 (sum 496 = total games).
Such outcomes are the transitive tournaments: 32! of them, out of 2^{C(32,2)}=2^{496}.
team-winners.solution: Distinct win totals must be exactly $0,1,\dots,31$, which forces a transitive ranking — there are $32!$ such outcomes out of $2^{\binom{32}{2}}=2^{496}$. So $P=\dfrac{32!}{2^{496}}$, giving $a+b=32+496=528.$

hints: List the 4-flip sequences with a maximal H-run of exactly 2: HHTT,THHT,TTHH (2 heads) and HHTH,HTHH (3 heads).
P(p)=3p²(1−p)²+2p³(1−p)=p²(1−p)(3−p); maximize.
over-3-heads.solution: The qualifying sequences are $HHTT,THHT,TTHH$ (two heads) and $HHTH,HTHH$ (three heads), so $P(p)=3p^2(1-p)^2+2p^3(1-p)=p^2(1-p)(3-p)$. Setting $P'(p)=0$ gives $2p^2-6p+3=0$, so $p=\dfrac{3-\sqrt3}{2}$ and $a+b+c=8.$

hints: You never play paper; play rock w.p. a, scissors w.p. 1−a. Opponent plays paper w.p. b, scissors w.p. 1−b.
Payoff P(a,b)=a+b−3ab; find the equilibrium.
a=b=1/3 gives P=1/3.
no-rock.solution: Drop paper; let you play rock w.p. $a$, scissors $1-a$, and the opponent paper w.p. $b$, scissors $1-b$. Your payoff is $P(a,b)=a(1-2b)+(1-a)b=a+b-3ab$. The equilibrium $a=b=\tfrac13$ gives $P=\tfrac13+\tfrac13-3\cdot\tfrac19=\tfrac13.$

hints: Gaps are 10 min normally, 70 min if the driver refills (prob 0.1) — the inspection paradox applies.
bus-wait-ii.solution: Inter-bus gaps are $L=10$ w.p. $0.9$ and $70$ w.p. $0.1$. Arriving at a random time, the expected wait (inspection paradox) is $\dfrac{\mathbb{E}[L^2]}{2\mathbb{E}[L]}$. With $\mathbb{E}[L]=16$ and $\mathbb{E}[L^2]=580$, this is $\dfrac{580}{32}=\dfrac{145}{8}.$

hints: Each dot lands on one of 4 edges uniformly; want all distinct.
placing-dots.solution: The first dot is anywhere; the second avoids its edge ($\tfrac34$) and the third avoids both ($\tfrac24$): $P=\tfrac44\cdot\tfrac34\cdot\tfrac24=\tfrac38.$

hints: Only parities matter: place five odds so every row, column, and diagonal has an odd count of odds.
The unique pattern is the central 'plus' (middle row + middle column).
1 valid placement of odds out of C(9,5)=126 ⇒ 1/126.
grid-filling-iii.solution: Each line needs an odd number of odd entries. Placing the five odd numbers in the central 'plus' (the middle row and middle column) makes every row, column, and both diagonals have an odd count — and it's the only such pattern. The odds occupy $1$ of $\binom95=126$ position-sets, so $P=\tfrac1{126}.$

hints: Similar triangles: lamp height/(x+s) = 6/s. Use the given x=10,s=15 to find the lamp height (10).
Then s = 1.5x, so ds/dt = 1.5·dx/dt.
casted-shadow.solution: Similar triangles give $\dfrac{H}{x+s}=\dfrac6s$; from $x=10,s=15$, $H=10$. Then $10s=6(x+s)\Rightarrow s=\tfrac32 x$, so $\dfrac{ds}{dt}=\tfrac32\dfrac{dx}{dt}=\tfrac32\cdot40=60$ ft/min (independent of position).

hints: Hours off = 24n × (number of days with at least one birthday).
E[off days]=365(1−(364/365)ⁿ) → 365, so E[hours off]≈24n·365 grows with n.
Unbounded in n ⇒ infinite ⇒ −1.
hours-of-labor.solution: On a day with at least one birthday all $n$ employees get $24$ hours off, so expected hours off $=24n\cdot365\big(1-(\tfrac{364}{365})^n\big)$. As $n\to\infty$ the bracket $\to1$ and the expression grows like $24\cdot365\,n\to\infty$. There is no maximizer, so the answer is $-1.$

hints: Now it's zero-sum: X's payoff is [x(100−x) − y(100−y)]/(x+y).
At the symmetric equilibrium x=y, ∂/∂x of the numerator gives (100−2x)(2x)=0.
optimal-marbles-ii.solution: X's expected payoff is $U(x,y)=\dfrac{x(100-x)-y(100-y)}{x+y}$ (X gains when its token is drawn, pays when Y's is). At the symmetric equilibrium $x=y$, $\partial_x U=0$ reduces to $(100-2x)(2x)=0$, so $x=50.$

hints: In the best case the sick count doubles each minute.
Need 2ᵗ ≥ 1000.
2¹⁰=1024 ⇒ 10 minutes.
infected-dinner-i.solution: At best every sick person infects a new healthy one each minute, so the count doubles: $1,2,4,\dots,2^t$. We need $2^t\ge1000$, giving $t=10$ (since $2^{10}=1024$).

hints: Each card is uniform on {0,1,2,3,4}: variance (5²−1)/12=2.
Independent sum ⇒ variance adds: 2+2=4.
deviating-sums.solution: Each card is uniform on $\{0,\dots,4\}$ with variance $\tfrac{5^2-1}{12}=2$. The independent sum has variance $2+2=4$, so the standard deviation is $2.$

hints: The allowed moves are cyclic rotations, so only the cyclic order matters.
Of the 6 orderings, the 3 in RGB's cyclic class can be rotated to RGB.
rainbow-trains.solution: Moving the back car to the front is a cyclic rotation, which preserves cyclic order. Of the $6$ arrangements, exactly the $3$ in the same cyclic class as $RGB$ can be rotated into rainbow order, so $P=\tfrac36=\tfrac12.$

hints: The earth card is irrelevant; consider only air, water, fire.
You win iff fire is last among these three.
P(fire last of 3)=1/3.
A car leads its own cluster iff it's the slowest among all cars ahead of it (a 'record' from the front).
P(car ranked i-th from front leads)=1/i, so E[K]=Σ_{i=1}^{N} 1/i = H_N.
car-crash.solution: A car heads its own cluster exactly when no car ahead of it is slower — i.e. it is a left-to-right minimum of speeds. The $i$-th car from the front is such a record with probability $\tfrac1i$, so $\mathbb{E}[K]=\sum_{i=1}^{N}\tfrac1i=H_N$. For $N=10$, $H_{10}=\tfrac{7381}{2520}.$

hints: Divisible by 10^80 needs both exponents in {80,…,99}: 20·20=400.
multiple-divisors-i.solution: $10^{99}=2^{99}5^{99}$ has $100\cdot100=10000$ divisors. Being divisible by $10^{80}=2^{80}5^{80}$ requires each exponent in $\{80,\dots,99\}$: $20\cdot20=400$ divisors. So $P=\tfrac{400}{10000}=\tfrac1{25}.$

hints: By Itô, d(Wⁿ)=nW^{n−1}dW + (n(n−1)/2)W^{n−2}dt; martingale needs zero drift.
Drift vanishes only for n=0 and n=1.
exponential-brownian-motion.solution: By Itô, $d(W_t^n)=nW_t^{n-1}dW_t+\tfrac{n(n-1)}2 W_t^{n-2}dt$, which is driftless (a martingale) only for $n=0$ and $n=1$. The sum of factorials is $0!+1!=2.$

hints: E[X²]=Var(X)+(E[X])².
Vega ∝ S·√T·φ(d₁); for ATM options it grows with the price level.
Higher strike means higher underlying (ATM), hence larger vega.
strike-vega.solution: For ATM options, vega $\approx S\sqrt T\,\varphi(d_1)$ scales with the price level $S=K$. The largest strike $K=30$ therefore has the greatest vega.

hints: Distinct pairwise digit sums means the digits form a Sidon (B₂) set within {0,…,9}.
The largest Sidon subset of {0,…,9} has size 5 (e.g. {0,1,2,6,9}).
So n=5.
pairwise-digit-sums-i.solution: All pairwise sums distinct means the digits form a Sidon set. The largest Sidon subset of $\{0,\dots,9\}$ has size $5$ (for instance $\{0,1,2,6,9\}$ has all $\binom52=10$ pairwise sums distinct), so the largest such number has $n=5$ digits.

hints: Choose a = max of f on [0,1]; f'(x)=0 at x=2/3 gives a=16/9.
Acceptance probability = (area under f)/(rectangle area)=1/(1·16/9)=9/16.
Expected rejects per accept = (1−p)/p = (7/16)/(9/16) = 7/9.
acceptancerejection-sampling.solution: The tightest rectangle uses $a=\max f=f(\tfrac23)=\tfrac{16}{9}$. Each point is accepted with probability $\dfrac{\int_0^1 f}{1\cdot a}=\dfrac{1}{16/9}=\dfrac9{16}$. The number of rejects before an accept is geometric with mean $\dfrac{1-p}{p}=\dfrac{7/16}{9/16}=\dfrac79.$

hints: The density is a Laplace distribution, symmetric about μ.
Symmetric distributions have zero third central moment.
Skewness = 0.
skewed.solution: The density $f(x)=\tfrac{\lambda}2 e^{-\lambda|x-\mu|}$ is symmetric about $\mu$, so all odd central moments vanish. Hence the skewness $\mathbb{E}[((X-\mu_X)/\sigma_X)^3]=0.$

hints: Each take must be preceded by a roll, so a 'cycle' = (rolls until ≥n) + 1 take.
Cycle length 20/(21−n)+1 actions; #cycles=100/(cycle); take value averages (20+n)/2.
Maximize over n: n=6 gives (300/7)·13 = 3900/7.
take-and-roll-ii.solution: Since each take needs a fresh roll, a cycle is (roll until $\ge n$) then take, of length $\tfrac{20}{21-n}+1$ actions, each yielding a take worth $\tfrac{20+n}2$. Expected payout $=\dfrac{100}{\tfrac{20}{21-n}+1}\cdot\dfrac{20+n}{2}$, maximized at $n=6$: $\dfrac{300}{7}\cdot13=\dfrac{3900}{7}.$

hints: There are n−5 possible starting positions for a 6-chain; each is increasing with probability 1/6!.
increasing-chains.solution: Each of the $n-5$ starting positions gives an increasing $6$-chain with probability $\tfrac1{6!}=\tfrac1{720}$, so $\mathbb{E}=\tfrac{n-5}{720}$. Setting this to $1$ gives $n=725.$

hints: Condition on N: P(no heads | N=n)=(1/2)ⁿ.
Player 1 wants the numbers far apart; Player 2 wants them close.
From any pure pair, one player can profitably deviate (P1 to an endpoint, P2 toward P1).
No pure-strategy equilibrium exists ⇒ 0.
uniform-equilibrium-i.solution: Player 1 maximizes $|a-b|$ and Player 2 minimizes it. For any fixed $(a,b)$, Player 2 can move toward $a$ to reduce the distance, or Player 1 can move to an endpoint to increase it — so no pair is stable. There are $0$ pure-strategy Nash equilibria.

ols-review-iii.solution: Here $\bar x=0,\bar y=1$, $S_{xy}=7$, $S_{xx}=10$, so $\hat\beta_1=0.7,\hat\beta_0=1$. The residuals are $0.4,-0.3,0,-0.7,0.6$ with $\text{SSE}=1.10$, so $s^2=\dfrac{1.10}{5-2}=\dfrac{11}{30}.$

hints: Odd and divisible by 3 means ≡3 (mod 6).
counting-odds.solution: Numbers that are odd and divisible by $3$ are exactly those $\equiv3\pmod6$: $3,9,\dots,2019$. Their count is $\dfrac{2019-3}{6}+1=337.$

hints: Scale to 600 units: Alice 5/min, Bob 6/min. After 40 min they've built 440, leaving 160.
Three together finish 160 in 10 min: (5+6+c)·10=160 ⇒ c=5.
Charlie alone: 600/5 = 120 min.
builders.solution: Take the pipe as $600$ units: Alice builds $5$/min, Bob $6$/min. In $40$ min they finish $440$, leaving $160$, done in $10$ more minutes by all three: $(5+6+c)\cdot10=160\Rightarrow c=5$. So Charlie alone needs $600/5=120$ minutes.

hints: Starting 1 make, 1 miss, the made-fraction is a martingale fixed at 1/2 (Pólya urn).
So the expected fraction made over 100 attempts is 1/2.
basketball-practice-ii.solution: This is a Pólya urn: the running fraction made is a martingale equal to its start $\tfrac12$. Hence the expected number made in $100$ attempts is $100\cdot\tfrac12=50.$

hints: Doubling data: X'=2X so (X'ᵀX')⁻¹=(1/4)(XᵀX)⁻¹; but the y's (and errors) also double, so σ'²=4σ².
Var(β')=σ'²(X'ᵀX')⁻¹=4σ²·(1/4)(XᵀX)⁻¹.
= σ²(XᵀX)⁻¹ = Var(β) ⇒ c=1.
double-data-trouble-v.solution: With $X'=2X$, $(X'^TX')^{-1}=\tfrac14(X^TX)^{-1}$. But doubling the data also doubles the responses and errors, so $\sigma'^2=4\sigma^2$. Hence $\mathrm{Var}(\hat\beta')=4\sigma^2\cdot\tfrac14(X^TX)^{-1}=\sigma^2(X^TX)^{-1}=\mathrm{Var}(\hat\beta)$, giving $c=1.$

hints: Number of hearts is hypergeometric (n=8, K=13, N=52).
8-card-heart.solution: The count of hearts is hypergeometric, with $\mathrm{Var}=n\cdot\tfrac{K}{N}\big(1-\tfrac{K}{N}\big)\cdot\tfrac{N-n}{N-1}=8\cdot\tfrac14\cdot\tfrac34\cdot\tfrac{44}{51}=\dfrac{22}{17}.$

hints: For two coprime values a,b, the largest non-representable amount is the Frobenius number ab−a−b.
Here a=5, b=21.
stamp-sum.solution: For coprime denominations $5$ and $21$, the largest value not representable is the Frobenius number $5\cdot21-5-21=105-26=79.$

hints: Comparing two variances uses the F statistic = ratio of sample variances.
bolt-variance-ii.solution: The test statistic for comparing two variances is $F=\dfrac{s_1^2}{s_2^2}=\dfrac{0.0003}{0.0001}=3.$

hints: Test H₀:p=1/3 vs p>1/3 with p̂=0.4, n=1000.
Reject H₀ — yes, a preference (1).
statistical-test-review-iv.solution: Testing $p=\tfrac13$ against $p>\tfrac13$: $Z=\dfrac{0.4-\tfrac13}{\sqrt{\tfrac13\cdot\tfrac23/1000}}\approx4.47$, well beyond any standard critical value, so we reject $H_0$ — customers do prefer plan $A$: answer $1.$

hints: Relative speeds: d/(v_A−v_B)=7 and (Alice is 3d behind Carter) 3d/(v_A−v_C)=12.
Then v_B−v_C=(v_A−v_C)−(v_A−v_B)=d/4−d/7=3d/28; Bob reaches Carter at 2d/(v_B−v_C)=56/3 min.
After Alice passes Carter (12 min): 56/3−12=20/3 min = 400 s.
car-line.solution: From relative speeds: $v_A-v_B=\tfrac d7$ and $v_A-v_C=\tfrac{3d}{12}=\tfrac d4$, so $v_B-v_C=\tfrac d4-\tfrac d7=\tfrac{3d}{28}$. Bob (initially $2d$ behind Carter) catches Carter at $\tfrac{2d}{3d/28}=\tfrac{56}{3}$ min after the instant, i.e. $\tfrac{56}{3}-12=\tfrac{20}{3}$ min after Alice passes Carter $=400$ seconds.

hints: T~Unif(1,5); want P(T<3 | T>2).
Find the smallest coin count where 60¢ has two different (nickel,dime,quarter) breakdowns.
With 6 coins: (0 q, 6 d, 0 n) and (1 q, 2 d, 3 n) both total 60¢.
So N=6.
coin-identifier.solution: We need the fewest coins for which $60$¢ is ambiguous. With $6$ coins, both $6$ dimes and $\{1\text{ quarter},2\text{ dimes},3\text{ nickels}\}$ total $60$¢, so the counts can't be determined. No smaller $N$ is ambiguous, so $N=6.$

hints: Total shaded = cs; if every row had ≤ t−1, then cs ≤ r(t−1).
So c > r(t−1)/s forces some row to reach t: c = ⌊r(t−1)/s⌋ + 1.
minimal-shade.solution: If no row reaches $t$, every row has $\le t-1$ black squares, so $cs\le r(t-1)$. Thus $c=\big\lfloor\tfrac{r(t-1)}{s}\big\rfloor+1$ forces a row with $\ge t$. For $(30,21,14)$: $\lfloor\tfrac{600}{14}\rfloor+1=42+1=43.$

hints: Total value of all 8 bills = 200; each drawn bill averages 200/8=25.
E[sum of 7] = 7·25.
dollar-draw.solution: The eight bills total $\$200$, so each selected bill averages $\$25$. The expected sum of $7$ is $7\cdot25=\$175.$

hints: A path is an arrangement of 3 x-moves, 4 y-moves, 5 z-moves.
coordinate-jumper.solution: Each path is an ordering of $3+4+5=12$ unit moves: $\dfrac{12!}{3!\,4!\,5!}=27720.$

hints: Cov(U,V)=Cov(X+Y,X+Z)=Var(X)=64 (others uncorrelated).
Position k wins iff the first k−1 birthdays are distinct AND yours matches one of them.
The optimum is near √365 ≈ 19–20; it peaks at position 20.
free-sundae.solution: You win at position $k$ if the first $k-1$ people all have distinct birthdays and yours matches one of them: $P(k)=\Big(\prod_{i=1}^{k-2}\tfrac{365-i}{365}\Big)\cdot\tfrac{k-1}{365}$. Maximizing this discrete product (the optimum sits near $\sqrt{365}\approx19$) gives the peak at position $20.$

hints: The ratio L=lim F_{n+1}/F_n satisfies L=1+1/L.
fibonacci-limit.solution: Dividing $F_{n+1}=F_n+F_{n-1}$ by $F_n$ and taking the limit gives $L=1+\tfrac1L$, i.e. $L^2-L-1=0$, so $L=\tfrac{1+\sqrt5}{2}$. Thus $a=1,b=5,c=2$ and $abc=10.$

hints: Expected rolls for a run of k successes with prob p is (p^{−k}−1)/(1−p).
Here p=1/6, k=2.
rolls-in-a-row.solution: The expected number of rolls for two consecutive $6$s is $\dfrac{p^{-2}-1}{1-p}=\dfrac{36-1}{5/6}=42.$

hints: Only 4,5,6 matter (each 1/3); stop at two 5s (win) or both a 4 and a 6 (lose).
Track (number of 5s, whether one of {4,6} seen); solve the win-probability equations.
doubly-5-ii.solution: Conditioning on rolls in $\{4,5,6\}$ (each $\tfrac13$), track the number of $5$s and whether a $4$ or $6$ has appeared. Solving the win-probability system gives state values $w_C=\tfrac12,\,w_A=\tfrac23,\,w_B=\tfrac14$, so from the start $w=\tfrac13\cdot\tfrac23+\tfrac23\cdot\tfrac14=\tfrac{7}{18}.$

hints: The walk on the cube's 8 vertices is symmetric (regular degree 3), so the stationary distribution is uniform.
Expected return time to a state = 1/(stationary probability).
confused-ant-ii.solution: The walk on the cube is on $8$ vertices, each of degree $3$, so the stationary distribution is uniform with $\pi_v=\tfrac18$. The mean return time to the start is $1/\pi_v=8.$

hints: The median has mean 3.5 but smaller variance than a single die, so E[median²]=12.25+Var(median) lies between the two.
Order: b > c > a ⇒ 231.
die-rank.solution: $\mathbb{E}[X_1X_2]=3.5^2=12.25$ (a) and $\mathbb{E}[X_1^2]=\tfrac{91}{6}\approx15.17$ (b). The median has the same mean $3.5$ but smaller variance, so $\mathbb{E}[X_{(3)}^2]=12.25+\mathrm{Var}(X_{(3)})$ lies strictly between $12.25$ and $15.17$ (c). Thus $b>c>a$, i.e. $231.$

hints: The target dies iff the bomb lands within 1/2 mile of the center.
That area ratio is (1/2)²/1² = 1/4.
Unharmed: 1 − 1/4 = 3/4.
bombing-campaign.solution: The target is hit iff the bomb lands within $\tfrac12$ mile of the center, probability $\dfrac{(1/2)^2}{1^2}=\tfrac14$. So it survives with probability $1-\tfrac14=\tfrac34.$

hints: P(X)=1−2^{1−n}, P(Y)=(n+1)/2ⁿ, P(X∩Y)=n/2ⁿ (exactly one girl).
independent-children.solution: Here $P(X)=1-2^{1-n}$, $P(Y)=\tfrac{n+1}{2^n}$, and $P(X\cap Y)=\tfrac{n}{2^n}$ (exactly one girl). Independence requires $\tfrac{n}{2^n}=\big(1-2^{1-n}\big)\tfrac{n+1}{2^n}$, i.e. $2(n+1)=2^n$, satisfied uniquely by $n=3.$

hints: P(k balls)=(1/2)^k; P(no empty bin | k)=(3^k−3·2^k+3)/3^k.
empty-urn.solution: With $P(k)=(\tfrac12)^k$ and $P(\text{no empty}\mid k)=\tfrac{3^k-3\cdot2^k+3}{3^k}$ (inclusion–exclusion), the total is $\sum_k(\tfrac12)^k-3\sum_k(\tfrac13)^k+3\sum_k(\tfrac16)^k=1-\tfrac32+\tfrac35=\tfrac1{10}.$

hints: Semiannual coupon = 30, 10 periods, period rate 2.5%.
bond-practice-iii.solution: With semiannual coupons of $30$ over $10$ periods at $2.5\%$ per period: $PV=30\cdot\dfrac{1-1.025^{-10}}{0.025}+1000\cdot1.025^{-10}\approx262.56+781.20=1043.76.$

hints: Change is 100−x ∈ {1,…,99}; you need bills to make any value 1–99 as a subset sum.
exact-bills-ii.solution: The change is some value $1$–$99$, so you need subset sums covering $1$–$99$: four $\$1$ (to $0$–$4$), three $\$5$ (to $0$–$19$), four $\$20$ (to $0$–$99$). That's $4+3+4=11$ bills.

hints: Four-of-a-kind: 13 ranks × 48 choices for the 5th card.
poker-hands-i.solution: There are $13\cdot48=624$ four-of-a-kind hands out of $\binom{52}{5}=2598960$, so $p=\tfrac{624}{2598960}$ and $\tfrac1p=4165.$

hints: Forward +2, back −1, 9 motions, net 0: 2f−b=0 with f+b=9.
Gives f=3, b=6.
salah-goal.solution: To return to start with $9$ motions, $2f-b=0$ and $f+b=9$ give $f=3$ forwards, $b=6$ backs. So $P=\binom93(\tfrac12)^9=\tfrac{84}{512}=\tfrac{21}{128}.$

hints: Duplicating points gives X'ᵀX' = 2XᵀX, so (X'ᵀX')⁻¹ = (1/2)(XᵀX)⁻¹.
σ² is unchanged (same response values, same error variance).
double-data-trouble-ii.solution: Duplicating each point gives $X'^TX'=2X^TX$, so $(X'^TX')^{-1}=\tfrac12(X^TX)^{-1}$, while $\sigma^2$ is unchanged. Hence $\mathrm{Var}(\hat\beta')=\tfrac12\mathrm{Var}(\hat\beta)$ and $c=\tfrac12.$

hints: The die is symmetric under v ↔ 7−v, and so is the median.
A symmetric distribution about 3.5 has mean 3.5.
E[median]=7/2.
median-roll.solution: Replacing each face $v$ by $7-v$ leaves the dice distribution unchanged and maps the median to $7-\text{median}$. So the median is symmetric about $3.5$ and $\mathbb{E}[\text{median}]=\tfrac72.$

hints: Army speed 1, horseman s. Forward trip: 50/(s−1); back trip: 50/(s+1); total time = 50 (army walks 50).
hasty-horseman.solution: Let the army move at speed $1$ and the horseman at $s$. The forward and back legs take $\tfrac{50}{s-1}$ and $\tfrac{50}{s+1}$, summing to the total time $50$: $\tfrac1{s-1}+\tfrac1{s+1}=1\Rightarrow s^2-2s-1=0\Rightarrow s=1+\sqrt2$. The horseman travels $50s=50(1+\sqrt2)$, so $abc=50\cdot1\cdot2=100.$

hints: Each large loaf becomes two smalls (one eaten immediately, one returned to be eaten later).
Use a 'first ace' argument on the successive appearances of the large loaves to count smalls eaten between them.
Sum the contributions plus the small eaten from each large.
Inscribe the coin (cylinder, radius 1, thickness h) in a concentric sphere; 'side' = the spherical zone between the two faces.
By Archimedes, zone area ∝ height, so P(side)=h/(2R) with R=√(1+(h/2)²).
thick-coin.solution: Inscribe the coin in a concentric sphere; landing on its side corresponds to a spherical zone of height $h$, whose area is proportional to $h$ (Archimedes). So $P(\text{side})=\dfrac{h}{2R}$ with $R=\sqrt{1+(h/2)^2}$. Setting $\dfrac{h}{2\sqrt{1+h^2/4}}=\dfrac13$ gives $9h^2=4+h^2$, so $h^2=\tfrac12$ and $h=\tfrac1{\sqrt2}$, i.e. $k=2.$

hints: Group the 1000 people into bubbles; within a bubble everyone meets everyone over many rounds, while bubbles are paired across rounds.
Arrange so the infection spreads as slowly as possible (the worst case), placing the last healthy person far from the source.
Carefully count the rounds to reach the last person.
The first card fixes the rank; the second must match it.
3 of the remaining 51 cards share the rank.
first-pair.solution: Whatever the first card is, $3$ of the remaining $51$ cards share its rank, so $P=\tfrac3{51}=\tfrac1{17}.$

hints: T₁₀₀ has mean 25000 and variance 100·250²=6.25×10⁶.
cheese-lover-ii.solution: $T_{100}$ has mean $25000$ and variance $100\cdot250^2=6.25\times10^6$. By Chebyshev, $P(T>30000)\le P(|T-25000|>5000)\le\dfrac{6.25\times10^6}{5000^2}=\dfrac14.$

hints: When A finishes (1000m), B is at 800, so v_B/v_A=4/5; similarly v_C/v_B=4/5.
Distance left = 1000−640 = 360 m.
mcqueen-speeding.solution: When A finishes, B has gone $800$m, so $v_B/v_A=\tfrac45$; likewise $v_C/v_B=\tfrac45$. Thus $v_C/v_A=\tfrac{16}{25}$, putting C at $640$m. C is $1000-640=360$ meters from finishing.

hints: Inradius r = Area/s. With sides 16,16,24, s=28 and Area=48√7 (Heron).
largest-inscribed-circle.solution: With $s=28$, Heron gives Area $=\sqrt{28\cdot12\cdot12\cdot4}=48\sqrt7$. The inradius is $r=\tfrac{\text{Area}}{s}=\tfrac{48\sqrt7}{28}=\tfrac{12\sqrt7}{7}$, so the incircle area is $\pi r^2=\dfrac{144\pi}{7}$. Hence $p+q=144+7=151.$

hints: Equal profit means equal gross returns: 3A=4B=11C.
Smallest whole-dollar bets: set each to lcm(3,4,11)=132 ⇒ A=44,B=33,C=12.
Total staked 89, return 132, profit 132−89=43.
soccer-bets.solution: For equal profit in every outcome the gross returns must match: $3A=4B=11C$. The smallest whole-dollar bets set each to $\mathrm{lcm}(3,4,11)=132$, giving $A=44,B=33,C=12$ (total $\$89$). The guaranteed return is $\$132$, so the profit is $132-89=\$43.$

hints: To make the sum uniform on 1–12 with a normal 1–6 die, the other die must shift by 0 or 6.
Faces {0,0,0,6,6,6} give sums 1–6 and 7–12 each with probability 1/12.
Sum of faces = 3·0+3·6 = 18.
illegible-dice.solution: For the sum to be uniform on $1$–$12$, the illegible die must have faces $\{0,0,0,6,6,6\}$: adding $0$ gives sums $1$–$6$ and adding $6$ gives $7$–$12$, each value with probability $\tfrac1{12}$. The face sum is $3(0)+3(6)=18.$

hints: Digits are independent: d₉∈{8,9}, d₀∈{0,1}, dᵢ∈{i−1,i,i+1} for i=1..8.
Average = Σ E[dᵢ]·10ⁱ with E[dᵢ]=i (i=1..8), E[d₉]=8.5, E[d₀]=0.5.
digit-difference.solution: The digits are chosen independently with $\mathbb{E}[d_i]=i$ for $i=1..8$, $\mathbb{E}[d_9]=8.5$, $\mathbb{E}[d_0]=0.5$. So the average is $8.5\cdot10^9+\sum_{i=1}^{8}i\cdot10^i+0.5=8.5\cdot10^9+876543210+0.5=\dfrac{18753086421}{2}.$

hints: Track Julie's net (start 0); break even = back to 0 in 8 steps without hitting ±4 (bankruptcy).
Paths 0→0 in 8 steps: C(8,4)=70; subtract those touching +4 or −4 (1 each by reflection).
breaking-even.solution: Let the net change start at $0$; breaking even means returning to $0$ in $8$ steps without reaching $\pm4$ (a bankruptcy). There are $\binom84=70$ return paths; by reflection $1$ touches $+4$ and $1$ touches $-4$ (none both). So $70-2=68$ valid paths, giving $P=\dfrac{68}{256}=\dfrac{17}{64}.$

hints: The contract is a call struck at 40; only acquisition outcomes can exceed 40.
contracts-and-pricing-i.solution: The contract pays $\max(\text{price}-40,0)$. Only acquisition matters: $\mathbb{E}=0.2\big[0.5(10)+0.5\max(x-40,0)\big]=1+0.1\max(x-40,0)$. Setting this to $15$ gives $\max(x-40,0)=140$, so $x=180.$

hints: Work backwards from 409, inverting each raid (took a fraction plus a fractional sheep).
Before 3rd: (4c/5 − 3/5)=409 ⇒ c=512; before 2nd: 683; before 1st: solve 2a/3−1/3=683.
sheep-stealing.solution: Working backwards: after the third raid $\tfrac{4c}5-\tfrac35=409\Rightarrow c=512$; before the second $\tfrac{3b}4-\tfrac14=512\Rightarrow b=683$; before the first $\tfrac{2a}3-\tfrac13=683\Rightarrow a=1025.$

hints: Groups are the cycles of a random permutation; average group size = n / E[#cycles].
E[#cycles]=H_n; for n=50, H₅₀≈4.499.
party-groups-ii.solution: The groups are cycles of a random permutation, so the average group size is $\dfrac{n}{\mathbb{E}[\#\text{cycles}]}=\dfrac{50}{H_{50}}\approx\dfrac{50}{4.499}\approx11.1.$

hints: P(red from urn k)=1/2^k; posterior on urn k after a red ∝ (1/2^k).
P(second red)=Σ(1/2^k)²/Σ(1/2^k).
limited-urns.solution: Urn $k$ gives red with probability $\tfrac1{2^k}$. After a red, the posterior on urn $k$ is $\propto\tfrac1{2^k}$, so $P(\text{2nd red})=\dfrac{\sum_k(1/2^k)^2}{\sum_k 1/2^k}$. As $n\to\infty$ this is $\dfrac{\sum1/4^k}{\sum1/2^k}=\dfrac{1/3}{1}=\dfrac13.$

hints: P(says trader|trader)=2/3, P(says trader|researcher)=1/3; P(trader)=2/3.
Variance is maximized by pushing all mass to the extremes of [−1,1].
Y=±1 each with probability 1/2 has mean 0.
Var=1.
maximal-variance.solution: Variance on $[-1,1]$ is largest when the mass sits entirely at the endpoints: $Y=\pm1$ each w.p. $\tfrac12$ gives mean $0$ and $\mathrm{Var}(Y)=1$, which is the maximum.

hints: Every group of 3 must be missing some lock; assign one lock per 3-subset, held by the other 4.
n=C(7,3)=35 locks; each person holds locks for 3-subsets not containing them: m=C(6,3)=20.
private-documents.solution: For each of the $\binom73=35$ triples, create a lock that none of those three can open (the other four hold its key). Then any $4$ people together cover all locks, but no $3$ do, so $n=35$. Each person holds keys for triples excluding them: $m=\binom63=20$. Thus $10m+n=200+35=235.$

hints: The number of winning lines in n×n×n is ((n+2)³−n³)/2.
tic-tac-toe.solution: Embedding the $n^3$ board in an $(n+2)^3$ cube, the number of lines is $\dfrac{(n+2)^3-n^3}{2}$. For $n=4$: $\dfrac{216-64}{2}=76.$

hints: For the person knowing i others, P(knows ≥1 neighbour)=1 − (N−1−i)(N−2−i)/((N−1)(N−2)), N=2m.
Average over i=0..N−1; the sum of (N−1−i)(N−2−i) telescopes via Σj(j−1)=N(N−1)(N−2)/3.
familiar-neighbors.solution: Let $N=2m$. The person knowing $i$ others fails to know either neighbour with probability $\dfrac{(N-1-i)(N-2-i)}{(N-1)(N-2)}$. Averaging over $i$, $\dfrac1N\sum_i\dfrac{(N-1-i)(N-2-i)}{(N-1)(N-2)}=\dfrac1N\cdot\dfrac{N(N-1)(N-2)/3}{(N-1)(N-2)}=\dfrac13$. So the expected proportion knowing a neighbour is $1-\tfrac13=\tfrac23.$

hints: Choose exactly 2 of {1,2,3}, then anything from the rest.
mixed-set-i.solution: Pick $2$ of $\{1,2,3\}$ ($\binom32=3$ ways) and any subset of the remaining $7$ elements ($2^7=128$): $3\cdot128=384.$

hints: Each term is x₁^{e₁}…x₆^{e₆} with e₁+…+e₆=18.
Count nonnegative solutions: stars and bars.
multinomial-expansion.solution: Distinct terms correspond to exponent tuples with $e_1+\dots+e_6=18$, of which there are $\binom{18+5}{5}=\binom{23}{5}=33649.$

hints: 3 sq yd ~ Poisson(12).
poisson-review-iv.solution: Over $3$ square yards imperfections are $\mathrm{Poisson}(12)$, so $P(\ge1)=1-e^{-12}=1+\dfrac{-1}{e^{12}}$. Thus $a=-1,b=12$ and $a+b=11.$

hints: By radial symmetry the angle is uniform; P(X+Y>0)=1/2.
X>0 and X+Y>0 is the angular wedge from −45° to 90° (135°).
conditionally-normal.solution: By radial symmetry the direction is uniform. $X+Y>0$ is a half-plane (prob $\tfrac12$), and $X>0\cap X+Y>0$ is the wedge of angles $(-45^\circ,90^\circ)$, i.e. $135^\circ=\tfrac38$ of the circle. So the conditional probability is $\dfrac{3/8}{1/2}=\dfrac34.$

hints: List the 4 equally likely outcomes and their payoffs.
x³ ends in 11 ⟺ x³ ≡ 11 (mod 100); only x mod 100 matters.
Cubing is a bijection mod 25 (gcd(3,20)=1) and forces a unique residue mod 4, hence mod 100.
Exactly 1 of 100 residues works ⇒ 1/100 = 0.01.
magic-11.solution: We need $x^3\equiv11\pmod{100}$, which depends only on $x\bmod100$. Mod $25$, cubing is a bijection (since $\gcd(3,20)=1$), giving one solution; combined with the forced residue mod $4$, exactly one residue mod $100$ works. So the probability is $\tfrac1{100}=0.01.$

hints: By symmetry P(B)=P(C), and P(B)+P(C)+P(E)=1, so we need P(E)=1/3.
P(X>w)=1−w for w∈[0,1], so P(X>YZ)=E[1−YZ].
Characteristic: λ²−(trace)λ+det=0; trace=−1, det=−6.
basic-eigenvalues.solution: The characteristic equation is $\lambda^2-(\mathrm{tr})\lambda+\det=\lambda^2+\lambda-6=0$, so $\lambda=2,-3$. Then $a^2+b^2=4+9=13.$

hints: Model strength as balls (or Exp lifetimes); winner inheriting strength means the overall winner is decided by total strength.
Bob wins with probability (Bob's total)/(grand total).
colosseum-fight.solution: Represent each unit of strength as a ball; the rule 'winner with prob $\tfrac{a}{a+b}$ and inherits strength' makes the tournament equivalent to drawing balls so that the side owning the very last ball wins. That side is Bob with probability $\dfrac{\text{Bob's total}}{\text{grand total}}=\dfrac{30}{40}=\dfrac34.$

hints: Track heads−tails; it's a symmetric walk with absorbing barriers +10 and −7.
Expected time to hit a barrier from 0 is a·b.
coin-streak.solution: The difference heads$-$tails is a symmetric walk absorbed at $+10$ and $-7$. Starting at $0$, the expected time to hit a barrier is $a\cdot b=10\cdot7=70.$

hints: Put one good candy alone in a box; the rest (4 good, 5 bad) in the other.
picking-candy.solution: Place a single good candy in one box and the other $4$ good $+5$ bad in the second. Then $P(\text{good})=\tfrac12\cdot1+\tfrac12\cdot\tfrac49=\tfrac9{18}+\tfrac4{18}=\tfrac{13}{18}.$

hints: Cov(Z,Z²)=E[Z³]−E[Z]E[Z²].
All odd moments of a standard normal are 0.
Treat each collision as the two ants passing through each other.
Then every left-moving ant passes every right-moving ant exactly once.
Collisions = 40·80 = 3200.
ant-collision-ii.solution: Treating collisions as pass-throughs, each of Alice's $40$ ants crosses each of Bob's $80$ ants exactly once, so there are $40\times80=3200$ collisions.

hints: Complement: all three pieces exceed 0.05.
P(all pieces > a) = (1−3a)² for two cuts.
short-wood.solution: For two uniform cuts, the probability all three pieces exceed $a$ is $(1-3a)^2$. With $a=0.05$: $(1-0.15)^2=0.7225$, so $P(\text{shortest}\le0.05)=1-0.7225=0.2775=\dfrac{111}{400}.$

hints: Correlation is scale-invariant; signs of the slopes flip it.
Y₁=1+2X₁ (slope +), Y₂=3−4X₂ (slope −): sign is negative.
covariance-review-iv.solution: Correlation is invariant to positive scaling and flips sign with a negative slope. The slopes $+2$ and $-4$ give a sign of $-1$, so $\rho_{Y_1,Y_2}=-0.2.$

hints: Re-rolling doesn't change relative odds; condition on a decisive roll.
double-dice-payoff.solution: Conditioning on a decisive roll, double-$6$ has probability $\tfrac1{11}$ and a $6$-with-non-$6$ has $\tfrac{10}{11}$. The game is fair when $\tfrac1{11}(100)-\tfrac{10}{11}x\ge0$, i.e. $x\le10$. So the maximum is $x=10.$

hints: For each first roll k, compute the reroll value (pay k if sum≥7, else get the 2nd roll).
Reroll beats keeping only for k=1 (value 7/3); keep 2,3,4,5,6.
unlucky-seven-i.solution: For first roll $k$, the reroll's expected value is $\tfrac16\sum_j[\,j\text{ if }k+j<7\text{ else }-k\,]$, which only beats keeping when $k=1$ (worth $\tfrac73$). So the optimal payoff per first roll is $\tfrac73,2,3,4,5,6$, giving $\mathbb{E}=\tfrac16\big(\tfrac73+20\big)=\tfrac{67}{18}.$

hints: The error e_n=G_n−F_n satisfies the same Fibonacci recurrence with e_100=1, e_99=0.
Each weighing gives 3 outcomes, so k weighings distinguish 3^k cases.
There are 90·2=180 possibilities (which bean, heavier/lighter).
balanced-beans-iv.solution: Each weighing has $3$ outcomes, distinguishing at most $3^k$ cases, and there are $90\times2=180$ possibilities. Since $3^4=81<180\le243=3^5$, the minimum is $5$ weighings.

hints: First return of a ±1 walk to 0 at step 2n has probability (1/(2n−1))C(2n,n)(pq)ⁿ.
