finite-coin-equalizer.solution: The first time heads and tails are equal corresponds to a $\pm1$ walk's first return to $0$, which occurs at step $2n$ with probability $\dfrac{1}{2n-1}\binom{2n}{n}(pq)^n$. For $p=\tfrac13,n=5$: $\dfrac{1}{9}\binom{10}{5}(\tfrac29)^5=\dfrac{252}{9}\cdot\dfrac{32}{59049}\approx0.0152.$

hints: All three odds before any even ⟺ the three odds are the first three values to appear among all six.
First-appearance order of the six faces is uniformly random.
even-before-odd.solution: We need all three odd faces to appear before any even face — i.e. the three odds occupy the first three positions in the (uniformly random) order of first appearances of the six faces. That probability is $\dfrac{3!\,3!}{6!}=\dfrac{36}{720}=\dfrac1{20}.$

hints: Each element has 3 options: exclude, include, or bold-include.
6 independent elements.
bold-subsets.solution: Each of the $6$ elements is independently excluded, plainly included, or bold-included — $3$ choices each — giving $3^6=729$ sets.

hints: R²=1−SSE/SST; duplicating data doubles both SSE and SST.
The ratio SSE/SST is unchanged.
R² stays 0.56.
double-data-trouble-i.solution: Duplicating each point doubles both $\text{SSE}$ and $\text{SST}$, leaving $R^2=1-\tfrac{\text{SSE}}{\text{SST}}$ unchanged at $0.56.$

hints: Optimal play clusters all 'place' actions first, then all 'take' actions.
After p places, the two boxes hold random amounts summing to p; t=100−p takes empty random boxes (re-taking an empty box yields 0).
Write expected payoff as a function of p and maximize.
The limit point is the sum of the independent left-endpoint increments at each step.
Step k adds an independent U(0,(1/2)ᵏ), with variance ((1/2)ᵏ)²/12.
convergent-intervals.solution: The convergence point is $P=\sum_{k\ge1}U_k$ where $U_k\sim U(0,(1/2)^k)$ are independent, with $\mathrm{Var}(U_k)=\dfrac{(1/4)^k}{12}$. Summing, $\mathrm{Var}(P)=\dfrac1{12}\sum_{k\ge1}(1/4)^k=\dfrac1{12}\cdot\dfrac13=\dfrac1{36}.$

hints: Split the hour into two independent 30-minute halves with crash probability p each.
No crash in the hour = no crash in both halves: (1−p)²=1/9.
car-crashes.solution: Let $p$ be the probability of a crash in $30$ minutes. The two halves are independent, so $(1-p)^2=P(\text{no crash in hour})=\tfrac19$, giving $1-p=\tfrac13$ and $p=\tfrac23.$

hints: p_k = ½p_{k−1} + ½p_{k−2}, with p_0=1.
Largest is at k=2: 2/3 + 1/12 = 3/4.
leap-frog.solution: Position $k$ is reached from $k-1$ or $k-2$, so $p_k=\tfrac12p_{k-1}+\tfrac12p_{k-2}$ with $p_0=1$. The solution is $p_k=\tfrac23+\tfrac13(-\tfrac12)^k$, maximized at $k=2$: $p_2=\tfrac23+\tfrac1{12}=\tfrac34.$

hints: β = P(accept H₀ | p=0.7) = P(X not in rejection region).
Acceptance region |x−18|<4 means 15 ≤ X ≤ 21, with X~Bin(36,0.7).
statistical-test-review-ii.solution: $\beta$ is the probability of accepting $H_0$ when $p=0.7$, i.e. $X\in\{15,\dots,21\}$ for $X\sim\mathrm{Bin}(36,0.7)$. Summing, $\beta=\sum_{k=15}^{21}\binom{36}{k}(0.7)^k(0.3)^{36-k}\approx0.0915.$

hints: Each draw-and-refill multiplies the wine fraction by (10−x)/10.
keg-of-wine.solution: Each ladle-and-refill scales the wine fraction by $\tfrac{10-x}{10}$, so after two days $\big(\tfrac{10-x}{10}\big)^2=\tfrac12$, giving $10-x=5\sqrt2$ and $x=10-5\sqrt2$. With $a=10,b=5,c=2$ (and $a=bc$), $a+b+c=17.$

hints: P(sum 7)=2[p₁p₆+p₂p₅+p₃p₄] as a function of x.
Differentiate the bracket: −1/6 + (3/10 − 4x) = 0.
weird-die.solution: $P(7)=2\big[\tfrac1{36}+\tfrac{1/5-x}{6}+(\tfrac1{10}+2x)(\tfrac15-x)\big]$. Differentiating the bracket gives $-\tfrac16+(\tfrac3{10}-4x)=0$, so $\tfrac2{15}=4x$ and $x=\tfrac1{30}.$

hints: With middle age m and spacing s, Σ(m+is)² for i=−4..4 = 9m²+60s².
Need 9m²+60s² = d² with d ≤ 60.
consecutive-children.solution: The nine ages are $m+is$, $i=-4..4$, with $\sum(m+is)^2=9m^2+60s^2=d^2$. Searching $d\le60$, $d=48$ works: $2304=9(14)^2+60(3)^2$, i.e. $m=14,s=3$ giving ages $2,5,\dots,26$. The father is $48.$

hints: All intervals share a point ⟺ every left endpoint precedes every right endpoint.
With n=5 intervals and 10 points, this forces the 5 smallest points to be the lefts: matchings = n!, total = (2n−1)!!.
intersecting-intervals.solution: A common point exists iff $\max(\text{lefts})<\min(\text{rights})$, i.e. the $5$ smallest of the $10$ points are all left endpoints. Such pairings number $5!$ out of $(2\cdot5-1)!!=945$ matchings, so $P=\dfrac{120}{945}=\dfrac{8}{63}$ and $p+q=71.$

hints: First find the Δ of the K=6 call (use Γ to move from the ATM Δ≈0.5).
Plug that Δ into σ(x)=(x−0.5)²+0.3.
The implied vol rounds to 0.31.
The first sock is any color; only the second matters.
Of the 19 remaining socks, exactly 1 matches.
colorful-socks-i.solution: After drawing any first sock, exactly $1$ of the remaining $19$ socks matches its color, so $P=\tfrac1{19}.$

hints: Adding all pair sums gives 2(w₁+w₂+w₃)=19+21+28=68, so the total is 34.
Each weight = 34 − (the pair sum excluding it): 15, 13, 6.
paired-pumpkins.solution: Summing the pair masses, $2(w_1+w_2+w_3)=68$, so the total is $34$. Each weight is $34$ minus the opposite pair: $15,13,6$. Then $w_1^2+w_2^2+w_3^2=225+169+36=430.$

hints: Set up states by the current trailing run; let μ be expected additional flips.
Solve μ_H=1+½μ_T+½μ_HH etc.; by symmetry the post-first-flip expectation is 6.
Add the first flip: 7.
3-heads-3-tails-ii.solution: With states for the current trailing run, $\mu_H=1+\tfrac12\mu_T+\tfrac12\mu_{HH}$, $\mu_{HH}=1+\tfrac12\mu_T$, and symmetric equations for tails. Solving gives $\mu_H=\mu_T=6$, so after the first flip $6$ more are expected; total $=7.$

hints: Only the relative order of the 4 DaBaby and 6 Rae songs matters.
All Rae before the 2nd DaBaby ⟺ at least 3 DaBaby songs come after the last Rae.
Favorable D-position sets: 7 of C(10,4)=210 ⇒ 1/30.
playlist.solution: Among the $4$ DaBaby ($D$) and $6$ Rae ($R$) songs, the event is that the $2$nd $D$ falls after the last $R$, i.e. at least $3$ $D$'s trail all $R$'s. Counting $D$-position sets in $\binom{10}{4}=210$: all four trailing ($1$) plus exactly three trailing with one $D$ among the first six ($6$) gives $7$, so $P=\tfrac{7}{210}=\tfrac1{30}.$

hints: Correlation is invariant to positive scaling and shifts.
8X scales positively; +7 is a shift.
Correlation stays 0.56.
defining-correlation.solution: Correlation is unchanged by positive scaling and additive shifts, so $\mathrm{corr}(8X,Y+7)=\mathrm{corr}(X,Y)=0.56.$

hints: After the first flip, each new flip stops the game (matches previous) w.p. 1/2.
P(stop at toss k) = (1/2)^{k−1} for k≥2.
even-doubles.solution: The game stops at toss $k$ if the first $k-1$ flips alternate and the $k$-th matches the previous, with probability $(\tfrac12)^{k-1}$ for $k\ge2$. Summing over even $k$: $\tfrac12+\tfrac18+\cdots=\dfrac{1/2}{1-1/4}=\dfrac23.$

hints: The top two meet in the final iff they start in opposite halves of the 128-player bracket.
Given player 1's slot, 64 of the other 127 slots are in the opposite half.
chess-tournament-i.solution: The two strongest players reach the final iff they are in different halves of the draw. Fixing the top seed, $64$ of the remaining $127$ positions lie in the opposite half, so $P=\tfrac{64}{127}.$

hints: Trump wins ⟺ he wins all three states, so 'PA Republican' pays 1 whenever 'Trump yes' does — and sometimes more.
Short Trump-yes (+0.17) and buy PA-Republican (−0.16); payoffs never net negative.
presidential-options.solution: Since Trump wins only if he wins all three states, 'PA Republican' pays $\$1$ in every state where 'Trump yes' pays $\$1$, and possibly more. Shorting Trump-yes (credit $0.17$) and buying PA-Republican (cost $0.16$) is never net-negative at expiry, for a riskless credit of $0.17-0.16=\$0.01.$

hints: Need x₁,x₂ in different sub-brackets: prob 2^{n−1}/(2ⁿ−1).
Each wins its own n−1 rounds (each as favorite, prob p): p^{n−1} each; then x₂ beats x₁ w.p. 1−p.
chess-tournament-iii.solution: They meet in the final with probability $\dfrac{2^{n-1}}{2^n-1}$. Each then wins its own $n-1$ rounds as the favorite ($p^{n-1}$ each), and $x_2$ beats $x_1$ with probability $1-p$. So the probability is $\dfrac{2^{n-1}}{2^n-1}p^{2(n-1)}(1-p)$; at $n=4,p=\tfrac34$: $\dfrac{8}{15}\big(\tfrac{27}{64}\big)^2\tfrac14=\dfrac{243}{10240}.$

hints: The paid value is uniform on {2,3,4,5,6} (conditioned on not being 1).
Average those five values.
limiting-values-i.solution: The last roll is uniform on $\{2,3,4,5,6\}$, so the fair value is $\dfrac{2+3+4+5+6}{5}=4.$

hints: Let e_i be expected rounds from i distinct faces; each round rolls 2 dice gaining 0, 1, or 2 new faces.
e_i = 1 + P₀e_i + P₁e_{i+1} + P₂e_{i+2}, with the gain probabilities from i.
Solving down from e₆=0 gives e₀ = 70219/9240.
dual-die-view.solution: Let $e_i$ be the expected rounds with $i$ faces collected. Rolling two dice from state $i$ gains $0,1,2$ new faces with probabilities $\tfrac{i^2}{36},\,\tfrac{(6-i)(1+2i)}{36},\,\tfrac{(6-i)(5-i)}{36}$, giving $e_i=1+P_0e_i+P_1e_{i+1}+P_2e_{i+2}$. Solving from $e_6=0$ yields $e_0=\dfrac{70219}{9240}.$

hints: After n rolls the payout is αⁿ with probability (5/6)ⁿ, else 0.
Expected payout (5/6)ⁿαⁿ is constant (=1) only if α(5/6)=1.
choose-your-profit.solution: After $n$ rolls the expected payout is $\alpha^n(\tfrac56)^n$. For this to stay constant at the starting value $1$ for all $n$, we need $\tfrac56\alpha=1$, i.e. $\alpha=\tfrac65.$

hints: Var(Y)=25c²+100(1−c)² by independence.
Differentiate: 50c−200(1−c)=0.
minimal-variance.solution: By independence $\mathrm{Var}(Y)=25c^2+100(1-c)^2$. Setting the derivative $50c-200(1-c)=0$ gives $250c=200$, so $c=\tfrac45.$

hints: Count length-n sequences whose first HH is at the end: these number F_{n−1} (Fibonacci).
new-question.solution: The number of length-$n$ sequences in which the first 'HH' occurs exactly at the end is the Fibonacci number $F_{n-1}$, so $P=\dfrac{F_{n-1}}{2^n}$. For $n=10$, $F_9=34$, giving $\dfrac{34}{1024}=\dfrac{17}{512}.$

hints: The fraction of red cards remaining is a martingale.
Any stopping rule has expected success equal to the starting red fraction.
= 26/52 = 1/2; no strategy beats it.
red-card-deal.solution: The proportion of red cards remaining in the deck is a martingale, so by optional stopping every strategy yields expected win probability equal to the initial red fraction, $\tfrac{26}{52}=\tfrac12$. No rule can do better, so the answer is $\tfrac12.$

hints: Each ant picks one of 2 endpoints: 2³=8 outcomes.
No collision only if all ants circulate the same way (all clockwise or all counter-clockwise).
ants-on-a-triangle.solution: Each ant chooses one of two adjacent vertices ($2^3=8$ outcomes). No two share a corner only when all three move the same rotational direction — clockwise or counter-clockwise — which is $2$ outcomes. So $P=\tfrac28=\tfrac14.$

hints: Runs = 1 + (number of adjacent pairs with different colors).
Each of the 99 adjacent pairs differs with probability 2·50·50/(100·99)=50/99.
marble-runs.solution: The number of runs equals $1$ plus the number of adjacent color changes. Each of the $99$ adjacent pairs has different colors with probability $\dfrac{2\cdot50\cdot50}{100\cdot99}=\dfrac{50}{99}$, so by linearity $\mathbb{E}[\text{runs}]=1+99\cdot\tfrac{50}{99}=51.$

hints: Let A be his age: A(1/4+1/5+1/3) + 13 = A.
stages-of-life.solution: With age $A$, $A(\tfrac14+\tfrac15+\tfrac13)+13=A$. Since $\tfrac14+\tfrac15+\tfrac13=\tfrac{47}{60}$, the remaining $\tfrac{13}{60}A=13$, so $A=60.$

hints: Try 1+r+r²+… for small ratios r>1 and at least 3 terms.
121 = 11², the smallest such square.
geometrical-progression.solution: Taking ratio $r=3$ with five terms, $1+3+9+27+81=121=11^2$, a perfect square. This is the smallest square achievable by such a progression, so the answer is $121.$

hints: Averaging 60 mph over 2 miles needs total time 2 minutes.
The first mile at 30 mph already takes 2 minutes.
No time remains for lap 2 ⇒ impossible ⇒ −1.
mile-rate.solution: An average of $60$ mph over $2$ miles requires a total time of $2$ minutes, but the first mile at $30$ mph already consumes $2$ minutes. No speed can complete the second lap in zero time, so the answer is $-1.$

hints: Monday = 2, so add 2.
2 + 2 = 4 (Wednesday).
mod-mondays.solution: Since $100\equiv2\pmod7$, the day is $2$ past Monday: $2+2=4.$

hints: The coupon rate (6%) equals the market rate.
A bond priced at a yield equal to its coupon trades at par.
Price = par = 1000.
bond-practice-v.solution: With the coupon rate equal to the market yield ($6\%$), the bond trades at par, so the price equals the face value, $\$1000.$

hints: Turning a pair changes the head count by ±2 or 0, so its parity is fixed.
Even initial heads → reach 4; odd → reach 3.
coin-pair-i.solution: Flipping two coins changes the number of heads by an even amount, so its parity is invariant. From an even count you can reach $4$ heads, from an odd count $3$. The initial count is even or odd each with probability $\tfrac12$, so $\mathbb{E}=\tfrac12(4)+\tfrac12(3)=\tfrac72.$

hints: Model states as (barrel, 7-vessel, 5-vessel) and search for the shortest pour sequence.
Each fill, empty, or transfer between containers counts as one transaction.
The minimum is 17 transactions.
The adversary concentrates on whichever n minimizes pₙn², so you equalize pₙn² across n.
pₙ ∝ 1/n²; Σ1/n² = π²/6 forces pₙ = (6/π²)/n².
The guaranteed value is 6/π², so a=6,b=2,ab=12.
generous-banker.solution: The opponent will place all mass on the $n$ minimizing $p_n n^2$, so you maximize the minimum by making $p_n n^2$ constant: $p_n\propto\tfrac1{n^2}$. Normalizing, $p_n=\dfrac{6/\pi^2}{n^2}$ and the guaranteed payout is $p_n n^2=\dfrac{6}{\pi^2}$. Thus $a=6,b=2$ and $ab=12.$

hints: Bulb k is toggled once per divisor of k.
It ends on iff k has an odd number of divisors — i.e. k is a perfect square.
100-lights.solution: Bulb $k$ is flipped once for each divisor of $k$, so it ends on iff $k$ has an odd number of divisors, which happens exactly for perfect squares. There are $10$ squares ($1^2,\dots,10^2$) up to $100$, so $10$ lights are on.

hints: Ignore all rolls that aren't a 5 or a 2.
Sum 5 has 4 ways, sum 2 has 1 way.
which-sum-first.solution: Conditioning on a decisive roll (sum $5$ or $2$), sum $5$ has $4$ combinations and sum $2$ has $1$, so $P=\dfrac{4}{4+1}=\dfrac45.$

hints: Compare only the first n coins of each: P(Emilia more)=P(Ron more)=a, P(tie)=b.
If already ahead/behind on n coins, the extra coin can't change it; only a tie is broken by it (heads, prob 1/2).
extra-coin.solution: On the first $n$ coins each, let $a=P(\text{Emilia ahead})=P(\text{Ron ahead})$ and $b=P(\text{tie})$, with $2a+b=1$. Emilia's last coin only matters on a tie, which she wins w.p. $\tfrac12$. So $P=a+\tfrac b2=a+\tfrac{1-2a}{2}=\tfrac12.$

hints: Let X~U(0,½) (left) and Y~U(½,1) (right); want Y−X>1/3.
Writing p=X, q=Y−½ (both U(0,½)), the event is p−q<1/6.
fish-slice.solution: Scale $L=1$ with $X\sim U(0,\tfrac12)$ and $Y\sim U(\tfrac12,1)$. Setting $p=X,\,q=Y-\tfrac12$ (both $U(0,\tfrac12)$), $Y-X>\tfrac13\Leftrightarrow p-q<\tfrac16$. The complementary region $p\ge q+\tfrac16$ has area $\tfrac1{18}$ out of $\tfrac14$, i.e. probability $\tfrac29$. So $P=1-\tfrac29=\tfrac79.$

hints: Only the +30% branch finishes in the money: S=10.4, payoff 2.4.
The flat and −50% branches pay 0.
trinomial-call-pricing-i.solution: The call pays only on the $+30\%$ move ($S=10.4$, payoff $2.4$); the flat and $-50\%$ outcomes pay $0$. So the price is $0.2\times2.4=0.48.$

hints: Condition on the original pattern: all distinct, one pair, or all same.
Each pattern's reproduction probability: 6/216, 3/216, 1/216 respectively.
matching-die-trio.solution: By pattern: all-distinct originals (prob $\tfrac{120}{216}$) re-match w.p. $\tfrac6{216}$; one-pair (prob $\tfrac{90}{216}$) w.p. $\tfrac3{216}$; all-same (prob $\tfrac6{216}$) w.p. $\tfrac1{216}$. Total $=\dfrac{120\cdot6+90\cdot3+6\cdot1}{216^2}=\dfrac{996}{46656}=\dfrac{83}{3888}.$

hints: β(Y on X)=S_xy/S_xx; β(X on Y)=S_xy/S_yy.
Here S_xx=2 < S_yy=50 (y-values are more spread out).
Dividing by a larger denominator decreases β ⇒ −1.
swapping-x-and-y.solution: $\beta_{Y|X}=\tfrac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}}$ while $\beta_{X|Y}=\tfrac{S_{xy}}{S_{yy}}$. Since the $y$'s are more spread ($S_{yy}=50>S_{xx}=2$), the slope shrinks, so $\beta$ decreases: answer $-1.$

hints: x=50cosθ ⇒ dx/dt = −50 sinθ·dθ/dt.
dθ/dt = −4/(50·0.8) = −1/10 rad/min (decreasing at 1/10).
slippery-ladder-ii.solution: From $x=50\cos\theta$, $\tfrac{dx}{dt}=-50\sin\theta\,\tfrac{d\theta}{dt}$. At $x=30$, $y=40$, so $\sin\theta=0.8$ and $\tfrac{d\theta}{dt}=-\dfrac{4}{50(0.8)}=-\dfrac1{10}$ rad/min — decreasing at $\tfrac1{10}.$

hints: Each prime may appear in at most one element; pick elements to maximize the total.
Use big primes 11,13,17,19,23,29 directly; for {2,3,5,7} prefer 28=2²·7, 27, 25 over the bare primes.
prime-subset.solution: Pairwise-coprimality means each prime is used by at most one element. Take the large primes $11,13,17,19,23,29$ as-is, and for the small primes use $28=2^2\cdot7$, $27=3^3$, $25=5^2$ (better than the bare primes), plus $1$. The sum is $1+28+27+25+11+13+17+19+23+29=193.$

hints: Track states (7-vessel, 11-vessel); each fill/empty/pour is one transaction.
Repeatedly fill the 7 and pour into the 11, emptying the 11 when full.
The minimum is 14 transactions.
Payout = (number of 1–3 rolls) if you end on a 4/5, else 0.
Stops are 4/5 (cash, prob 2/3 of stops) or 6 (bust); E[continues]=1 (geometric, p=1/2).
dice-game.solution: Each roll continues (1–3) w.p. $\tfrac12$, so the number of continues before stopping has mean $1$. A stop is a cash-out (4/5) w.p. $\tfrac23$ and a bust (6) w.p. $\tfrac13$, independent of the count. The payout is the count if cashed, else $0$, so $\mathbb{E}=\tfrac23\cdot1=\tfrac23.$

hints: An ATM call has delta ≈ 0.5.
Use the Taylor expansion ΔC ≈ δ·ΔS + ½Γ·(ΔS)².
Add the change to the initial price 2.3.
Unfold the box into a flat plane to straighten the path along the surface.
Compare √((a+b)²+c²) over the three ways of pairing the sides.
Divide the shortest distance by the speed 2.5 and round.
Put-call parity: C − P = S − Ke^{−rt}, which is linear in S.
The linear term has zero second derivative, so Γ_call = Γ_put.
options-gamma.solution: By put-call parity $C-P=S-Ke^{-rt}$, whose second derivative in $S$ is zero. Hence the call and put share the same gamma: $\Gamma_{\text{put}}=0.02.$

hints: Because k is uniform on 0..8, each digit is equally likely a 1 or a 9.
So E[each digit]=(1+9)/2=5.
9-for-1.solution: Choosing $k$ uniformly makes each digit symmetric between $1$ and $9$, so $\mathbb{E}[\text{digit}]=5$. Hence $\mathbb{E}[X]=5(10^7+\cdots+1)=5\cdot11111111=55555555.$

hints: A first flip can't win; from each player's second flip on, they complete the pair w.p. 1/2.
From turn 3 it's alternating Bernoulli(1/2) trials, Alice first.
coin-toss-game-i.solution: Nobody can win on a first flip; thereafter each own-flip completes the H-and-T pair with probability $\tfrac12$. From Alice's second flip onward the winners alternate as Bernoulli$(\tfrac12)$ trials with Alice first: $P=\tfrac12+\tfrac14P$, giving $P=\tfrac23.$

hints: There are 6! labelings, but the rotation group of a cube has 24 elements.
Distinct labelings = 6!/24.
dice-labels.solution: There are $6!$ ways to assign the numbers, but $24$ rotations of the cube identify equivalent labelings, so the count is $\dfrac{6!}{24}=\dfrac{720}{24}=30.$

hints: You must draw 5 real bills in a row (a fake resets progress); each draw is real w.p. 1/2.
Expected draws for k consecutive successes = (p^{−k}−1)/(1−p).
paying-bail.solution: You need $5$ real bills in a row, each real with probability $\tfrac12$; a fake resets. The expected number of draws for $k$ consecutive successes is $\dfrac{p^{-k}-1}{1-p}=\dfrac{2^5-1}{1/2}=62.$

hints: The expected number of k-cycles in a random permutation is 1/k.
Sum over k=n+1..2n: Σ1/k → ln(2n)−ln(n).
Limit = ln 2, so q=2.
half-cycle.solution: A random permutation has $\tfrac1k$ expected $k$-cycles, so $\mathbb{E}[C_n]=\sum_{k=n+1}^{2n}\tfrac1k\to\ln(2n)-\ln(n)=\ln2$. Thus $q=2.$

hints: Fix X₁; P(nearest arc L≥x)=(1−x/π)^{19} for x∈(0,π).
E[L]=∫₀^π (1−x/π)^{19}dx = π/20 radians.
In degrees: 180/20 = 9.
nearest-circular-neighbor.solution: Fixing $X_1$, no other point lies within arc $x$ on either side with probability $(1-\tfrac{x}{\pi})^{19}$, so $\mathbb{E}[L]=\int_0^\pi(1-\tfrac{x}{\pi})^{19}dx=\tfrac{\pi}{20}$ radians $=\tfrac{180}{20}=9^\circ.$

hints: Each $5 adds change, each $10 needs change: it's a balanced-ballot (Dyck path) condition.
P(every prefix has #fives ≥ #tens) = 1/(n+1) with n=19.
game-time.solution: The cashier can always make change iff in every prefix the number of \$5 bills is at least the number of \$10 bills — a balanced ballot condition. With $n=19$ of each, the probability is the Catalan ratio $\dfrac1{n+1}=\dfrac1{20}.$

hints: Kelly fraction f* = p − q/b.
bet-size-i.solution: The Kelly criterion gives $f^*=p-\dfrac{q}{b}=0.75-\dfrac{0.25}{1}=0.5$, i.e. bet $50\%$ of the bankroll.

hints: min(S_T,25) = S_T − max(S_T−25,0).
Replicate: long the underlying, short the K=25 call.
price-an-option.solution: Since $\min(S_T,25)=S_T-\max(S_T-25,0)$, replicate by holding the underlying and shorting the $K=25$ call: price $=S_0-C_{25}=21-0.7=20.3.$

hints: P(double | n heads) = 1/(1 + 19/2ⁿ).
Require this ≥ 0.95 ⇒ 2ⁿ > 361.
confident-double-heads.solution: By Bayes, $P(\text{double}\mid n\text{ heads})=\dfrac{1}{1+19/2^n}$. Requiring $\ge\tfrac{19}{20}$ gives $2^n>361$, so the minimum is $n=9.$

hints: The forward replicates as long underlying minus K zero-coupon bonds.
pricing-a-forward.solution: A forward is replicated by holding the asset and borrowing the present value of $K$: price $=S_0-Ke^{-rT}=3-2e^{-0.04}\approx3-1.9216=1.08.$

hints: ATM straddle ≈ 2·S·σ·√T/√(2π).
From 1 blue (2 red), a draw turns a red blue w.p. 2/3 ⇒ mean 3/2 draws.
From 2 blue (1 red), success w.p. 1/3 ⇒ mean 3 draws.
replacement-orbs.solution: Going from $1$ to $2$ blue requires drawing one of the $2$ reds (prob $\tfrac23$), a geometric mean of $\tfrac32$ draws. From $2$ to $3$ blue needs the last red (prob $\tfrac13$), mean $3$. Total $=\tfrac32+3=\tfrac92.$

hints: Pooled variance s_p²=(19·22²+19·20²)/38=442, so s_p≈21.02.
Two-tailed p ≈ 0.106.
shopping-habits.solution: Pooled variance $s_p^2=\dfrac{19(22^2)+19(20^2)}{38}=442$, so $s_p\approx21.02$ and the standard error is $21.02\sqrt{\tfrac1{20}+\tfrac1{20}}\approx6.65$. Then $t=\dfrac{78-67}{6.65}\approx1.655$ on $38$ df, giving a two-tailed $p\approx0.106.$

hints: Factor 999919 = 991 × 1009 (both prime).
Cats × mice-per-cat with both >1 and mice-per-cat > cats.
991 cats each killing 1009 mice (1009 > 991).
cats-and-mice.solution: $999919=991\times1009$, both prime. With more than one cat, each killing more than one mouse and more mice than there are cats, the only fit is $991$ cats each killing $1009$ mice ($1009>991$). So there were $991$ cats.

hints: 7y≡10000 (mod 3) ⇒ y≡1, so minimal positive b=1.
full-solutions.solution: Reducing mod $3$, $y\equiv1$, so the minimal positive value is $b=1$. Then $3x=10000-7=9993$ gives $x=a=3331$. Hence $ab=3331.$

hints: σ²_{X+Y}=σ²_X+σ²_Y+2ρσ_Xσ_Y.
First passage to 0 from n in m=n+2k steps uses k up and n+k down moves.
f=(n/m)·C(m,k)·pᵏ(1−p)^{n+k} (ballot/hitting-time formula).
delayed-ruin.solution: Reaching $0$ for the first time in $m=n+2k$ steps requires $k$ wins and $n+k$ losses, and the ballot formula gives $f=\dfrac{n}{m}\binom{m}{k}p^k(1-p)^{n+k}$. For $n=5,k=3,p=\tfrac13$: $\dfrac5{11}\binom{11}{3}\big(\tfrac13\big)^3\big(\tfrac23\big)^8\approx0.1084.$

hints: Each step lands on a uniform node — identical to a 10-sided die (coupon collector).
Expected steps to see all 10 = 10·H₁₀.
graph-search.solution: Each step jumps to a uniformly random node, exactly the coupon-collector setup with $10$ coupons, so the expected number of steps is $10H_{10}=10\big(1+\tfrac12+\cdots+\tfrac1{10}\big)\approx29.29\approx29.$

hints: Sticky-strike: implied vol is pinned to the strike, independent of spot moves.
The K=13 vol doesn't change when S moves to 15.
σ stays 0.3.
sticky-strike.solution: Under a sticky-strike model the implied volatility is a function of the strike alone and does not move with the underlying. So the $K=13$ option keeps $\sigma=0.3.$

hints: Need no match Mon, no match Tue, match Wed.
Count favorable splits over all C(10,2)C(8,2)C(6,2) ordered selections.
sock-drawer-ii.solution: Requiring mismatched draws Monday and Tuesday and a matched draw Wednesday, counting favorable selections over all $\binom{10}{2}\binom82\binom62$ ordered triples gives $P=\dfrac{26}{315}.$

hints: Two chords with 4 distinct endpoints cross w.p. 1/3; adjacent segments share a vertex and can't cross interior.
Non-adjacent segment pairs: C(n,2) − n.
segment-traversal.solution: Two segments on distinct endpoints intersect inside with probability $\tfrac13$; the $n$ adjacent pairs share a vertex and don't. With $\binom n2-n$ eligible pairs, $\mathbb{E}=\dfrac{\binom n2-n}{3}$. For $n=12$: $\dfrac{66-12}{3}=18.$

hints: Statement n ('at most n−1 true') is true iff the true-count T ≤ n−1.
So exactly the statements n=T+1..100 are true: 100−T of them.
true-statement.solution: Statement $n$ is true exactly when the number of true statements $T$ satisfies $T\le n-1$, i.e. for $n\ge T+1$. That makes $100-T$ statements true, so $100-T=T$ and $T=50.$

hints: Rotations are isometries, hence invertible.
So rank=3 and nullity=0.
rotation-matrix-ranked.solution: A rotation in $\mathbb{R}^3$ is an isometry and therefore invertible, so $\mathrm{rank}=3$ and $\mathrm{null}=0$. Thus $3^2+0^2=9.$

hints: The groups are the cycles of a random permutation of 50.
Expected number of cycles = H₅₀ = 1+1/2+…+1/50.
party-groups.solution: Each guest points to the name drawn, forming a random permutation whose cycles are the groups. The expected number of cycles is $H_{50}=\sum_{i=1}^{50}\tfrac1i\approx4.499$, so about $4.5$ groups.

hints: For odd n, Σ over odd k of C(n,k) = 2^{n−1}.
Sum over odd n=1..999: 2⁰+2²+…+2⁹⁹⁸ = (4⁵⁰⁰−1)/3 = (2¹⁰⁰⁰−1)/3.
odd-coefficients.solution: For odd $n$, $\sum_{k\text{ odd}}\binom nk=2^{n-1}$. Summing over odd $n\le999$: $\sum_{j=0}^{499}4^j=\dfrac{4^{500}-1}{3}=\dfrac{2^{1000}-1}{3}$. With $b$ maximal, $a=2,b=1000,c=1,d=3$, so $abcd=6000.$

hints: A larger margin N favors the stronger player (law of large numbers).
With p>1/2, the win probability → 1 as N→∞.
Answer: 1.
win-by-2.solution: As the required margin $N$ grows, randomness averages out and the stronger player (with $p=0.6>0.5$) almost surely gets ahead, so the probability A wins tends to $1.$

hints: Person i is visible iff they're the tallest of the first i, probability 1/i.
visible-number-of-heads.solution: Person $i$ is visible iff tallest among the first $i$, with probability $\tfrac1i$. By linearity $\mathbb{E}[X]=\sum_{i=1}^{20}\tfrac1i=H_{20}\approx3.6.$

hints: Given exactly 3 heads, all arrangements are equally likely.
There are C(5,3)=10 such sequences.
head-tail-sequence.solution: Conditioned on $3$ heads, each of the $\binom53=10$ equally likely arrangements has probability $\tfrac1{10}$, including THHTH.

hints: Each player needs 2 heads; Alice's j-th flip is turn 2j−1, Bob's k-th is turn 2k.
Alice wins iff j ≤ k, with j,k ~ NegBinom(2,1/2).
Summing gives 16/27.
coin-toss-game-ii.solution: Each player's flips to reach $2$ heads are $\mathrm{NegBinom}(2,\tfrac12)$: $P(\text{flips}=m)=\tfrac{m-1}{2^m}$. Alice (going first) wins iff her count $j$ satisfies $j\le k$ for Bob's count $k$. Summing $\sum_j\tfrac{j-1}{2^j}\sum_{k\ge j}\tfrac{k-1}{2^k}$ gives $\tfrac{16}{27}.$

hints: Place 6 and 66; they're in different groups if one is in the 30-set and the other in the 70-set.
split-100.solution: Treating $6$ and $66$ as two random distinct positions, they land in different groups with probability $\dfrac{2\cdot30\cdot70}{100\cdot99}=\dfrac{4200}{9900}=\dfrac{14}{33}.$

hints: The non-representable positive integers (sums of 4s and 5s) are 1,2,3,6,7,11 (Frobenius number 11).
That's 6 exceptions.
4-and-5-sum.solution: With parts $4$ and $5$, the only positive integers not expressible are $1,2,3,6,7,11$ (the Frobenius number is $11$). Removing these $6$ exceptions from $1$–$1000$ leaves $1000-6=994.$

hints: For Gamma(shape k, scale θ): E[Xⁿ]=θⁿ·(k+n−1)!/(k−1)!.
Pieces are 5, X, 8−X with X~U(0,8); triangle inequalities give 3/2 < X < 13/2.
That interval has length 5.
bread-slicer-i.solution: With pieces $5,X,8-X$ ($X\sim U(0,8)$), the triangle inequalities reduce to $\tfrac32<X<\tfrac{13}2$, an interval of length $5$. So $P=\tfrac58.$

hints: Let m(x)=E[N_x]; condition on X₁ to get m(x)=1+∫₀ˣ m(x−t)dt.
Differentiating gives m'(x)=m(x), so m(x)=eˣ (m(0)=1).
sum-exceedance-i.solution: Let $m(x)=\mathbb{E}[N_x]$. Conditioning on $X_1$, $m(x)=1+\int_0^x m(x-t)\,dt$, so $m'(x)=m(x)$ with $m(0)=1$, giving $m(x)=e^x$. Thus $\mathbb{E}[N]=e^{\ln2}=2.$

hints: X−Y−Z is normal with mean 12−12−12=−12 and variance 12+12+12=36.
The chest value is Binomial(20,1/2) with mean 10; bidding above 10 is dominated.
Count pure equilibria by how many players bid at the top and the tie-break incentives.
Sum the cases over k top-bidders.
Going from i to i+1 distinct toys is geometric with success (5−i)/5.
collecting-toys-i.solution: Collecting the $(i{+}1)$-th new toy takes a geometric mean of $\tfrac{5}{5-i}$ boxes, so $\mathbb{E}=5\sum_{j=1}^{5}\tfrac1j=5\cdot\tfrac{137}{60}=\tfrac{137}{12}.$

hints: The left side has only prime factors 2 and 3; the right only 5.
No k works ⇒ probability 0.
up-or-down.solution: Price is unchanged iff $(1.2)^k(0.8)^{10-k}=1$, i.e. $6^k4^{10-k}=5^{10}$. The left has only factors $2,3$ and the right only $5$, so no $k$ satisfies it: the probability is $0.$

hints: Implied USD→GBP via EUR is 1.5/0.85≈1.76, but the direct rate is 1.8 — trade USD→GBP first.
Profit = 20.
arbitrage-detective-ii.solution: The direct $1\,\text{USD}=1.8\,\text{GBP}$ beats the cross rate via EUR. Cycling $1000\,\text{USD}\to1800\,\text{GBP}\to\tfrac{1800}{1.5}=1200\,\text{EUR}\to1200(0.85)=1020\,\text{USD}$ yields a profit of $\$20.$

hints: Friend (first) wins with prob p/(1−(1−p)(1−q)); set equal to 1/2.
even-steven.solution: The first player wins with probability $\dfrac{p}{1-(1-p)(1-q)}$. Setting this to $\tfrac12$ with $p=0.4$ gives $1-0.6(1-q)=0.8$, so $0.6(1-q)=0.2$ and $q=\tfrac23.$

hints: Unread at box 1 happens if mail is in box 1 but unnoticed (1/3·2/3) or in another box (2/3).
P(box1 | observation)=(2/9)/(2/9+6/9).
you-got-mail.solution: The observation (checked box 1, nothing read) occurs with probability $\tfrac13\cdot\tfrac23=\tfrac29$ if the mail is in box 1 (unnoticed) and $\tfrac23$ otherwise. By Bayes, $P(\text{box 1})=\dfrac{2/9}{2/9+6/9}=\dfrac14.$

hints: Binary search halves the range each guess.
Need 2ⁿ ≥ 1000.
highlow-guess.solution: Binary search halves the candidate range each guess, needing $2^n\ge1000$. Since $2^{10}=1024\ge1000>512$, the answer is $n=10.$

hints: Vega magnitude is largest at-the-money (S≈K).
Dropping S from 42 toward K=36 increases the call's vega magnitude.
But you're short the call, so your position's vega decreases (more negative): −1.
call-vega.solution: Vega is maximized near the money, so as $S$ falls from $42$ toward $K=36$ the call's vega grows. Since you sold the call, your position vega is negative and becomes more negative — it decreases, so the answer is $-1.$

hints: For choosing m of n labeled 1..n, E[max]=m(n+1)/(m+1).
largest-ball.solution: The expected maximum when choosing $m$ of $\{1,\dots,n\}$ is $\dfrac{m(n+1)}{m+1}$. With $m=10,n=20$: $\dfrac{10\cdot21}{11}=\dfrac{210}{11}.$

hints: Ask 'is the sequence in set A?'; then guess one element of the indicated side.
Either answer leaves a uniform set, so P(correct)=1/2¹⁰⁰+1/2¹⁰⁰.
careful-coin-question.solution: With one yes/no question you split the $2^{100}$ sequences into a set and its complement, then guess one element on the indicated side. Each correct guess contributes $\tfrac1{2^{100}}$, totaling $2\cdot2^{-100}=2^{-99}$. So $a=2,b=99$ and $ab=198.$

hints: No two same-gender adjacent forces strict alternation.
Two patterns: BGBG… or GBGB…; each has 5!·5! arrangements.
theater-seating.solution: With $5$ boys and $5$ girls, no two of the same gender adjacent means the row must alternate, giving $2$ gender patterns. Each fills with $5!\cdot5!$ orders, so $2\cdot120\cdot120=28800.$

hints: Radial density on the unit disk is f(r)=2r.
E[R]=∫₀¹ r·2r dr.
dart-distance.solution: For a uniform point on the unit disk the radius has density $f(r)=2r$, so $\mathbb{E}[R]=\int_0^1 2r^2\,dr=\tfrac23.$

hints: Segments are disjoint iff one pair is exactly the two smallest points.
The lowest point pairs with the 2nd-lowest with probability 1/3.
overlapping-segments.solution: The two segments fail to overlap only when one segment's endpoints are the two smallest of the four points, i.e. the lowest point is paired with the second-lowest — probability $\tfrac13$. So overlap occurs with probability $1-\tfrac13=\tfrac23.$

hints: As the writer you keep the $2.50 premium; you lose once S exceeds K.
Breakeven when payoff S−K equals the premium.
breakeven-price-ii.solution: The writer keeps the $\$2.50$ premium and starts losing when $S-K$ exceeds it, so breakeven is $K+\text{premium}=12+2.50=\$14.50.$

hints: A maximal black run of length L contributes ⌊L/5⌋ non-overlapping components.
Sum the expected contributions over all runs (or count 5-windows minus the overlap correction).
The exact expectation is 284785/1048576, so p+q=1333361.
coloring-components-iii.solution: Each maximal black run of length $L$ yields $\lfloor L/5\rfloor$ non-overlapping $5$-components. Summing the expected contribution across all runs of the $20$-cell line gives $\mathbb{E}=\dfrac{284785}{1048576}$, so $p+q=1333361.$

hints: Check the triangle inequality: 55+62 = 117.
The sides are degenerate — no actual triangle (zero area).
Cost = 0.
the-price-of-a-garden.solution: Since $55+62=117$, the 'triangle' is degenerate with zero area, so the cost is $\$0.$

hints: Subtract 1 from each die: yᵢ≥0 with Σyᵢ=4 (upper bound 5 never binds since 4<5).
Count nonnegative solutions: C(4+7,7).
eight-dice.solution: Writing each die as $1+y_i$ with $y_i\ge0$, we need $\sum y_i=4$ (the cap of $5$ never binds). The count is $\binom{4+7}{7}=\binom{11}{7}=330$, so $x=330.$

hints: Shift to U₁,U₂~U(0,1): E[|X−Y|]=E[|U₁−U₂|].
The mean absolute difference of two U(0,1) is 1/3.
Let Iᵢ indicate face i is red; E[T]=6·E[I₁] by symmetry.
P(a given face is red)=10²/10³=1/10.
better-in-red-i.solution: By symmetry $\mathbb{E}[T]=6\,P(\text{a given face red})$. Each face of the big cube paints $10^2$ of the $10^3$ small cubes, so $P=\tfrac1{10}$ and $\mathbb{E}[T]=\tfrac6{10}=\tfrac35.$

hints: Straddle value v=E[|S|] for S~N(0,1).
v²=2/π, so a=2.
straddle-output.solution: With $K=0$, the straddle is worth $v=\mathbb{E}[|S|]=\sqrt{2/\pi}$ for $S\sim N(0,1)$. Then $v^2=\dfrac2\pi$, so $a=2.$

hints: The heavy bean is known to be heavier, so each weighing has 3 outcomes locating it among thirds.
Need 3ᵏ ≥ 18.
3²=9<18≤27=3³ ⇒ 3 weighings.
balanced-beans-iii.solution: Since the odd bean is known to be heavier, each weighing's three outcomes narrow it to a third. As $3^2=9<18\le27=3^3$, the minimum is $3$ weighings.

hints: Condition on Jimmy's j: P(Simon>j)=(3000−j)/3000.
strictly-better.solution: Conditioning on Jimmy's number $j$, $P(\text{Simon}>j)=\tfrac{3000-j}{3000}$. Averaging over $j=1..1000$: $\dfrac{1}{1000\cdot3000}\sum_{j=1}^{1000}(3000-j)=\dfrac{2499500}{3000000}=\dfrac{4999}{6000}.$

hints: Let x cows ⇒ 2x chickens ⇒ 4x spiders.
x=11, spiders=4x=44.
leg-count.solution: With $x$ cows, $2x$ chickens, $4x$ spiders, the legs total $4x+2(2x)+8(4x)=40x=440$, so $x=11$ and there are $4x=44$ spiders.

hints: Bob's EV = 11p₁p₂ + 9(1−p₁)(1−p₂) − 5, linear in p₂.
Bob picks p₂∈{0,1}; need 11p₁−5≤0 and 4−9p₁≤0.
tricky-bob-i.solution: Bob's expected value is $11p_1p_2+9(1-p_1)(1-p_2)-5$, linear in $p_2$, so he chooses $p_2=1$ (needing $11p_1-5\le0$) or $p_2=0$ (needing $4-9p_1\le0$). Both hold iff $p_1\in[\tfrac49,\tfrac5{11}]$, so $b-a=\tfrac5{11}-\tfrac49=\tfrac1{99}.$

hints: Let R(s) be expected rolls to reach total ≥5 from s; R(4)=1.
sum-exceedance-iv.solution: Let $R(s)$ be the expected rolls to reach $\ge5$ from total $s$. Then $R(4)=1$, $R(3)=\tfrac65$, $R(2)=\tfrac{36}{25}$, $R(1)=\tfrac{216}{125}$, and $R(0)=1+\tfrac15\big(\tfrac{216}{125}+\tfrac{36}{25}+\tfrac65+1\big)=1+\tfrac{671}{625}=\tfrac{1296}{625}.$

hints: Player 49 only loses to 50; survival depends on whether 49 and 50 get involved early.
Casework: 49 not yet played by round 11, or 49 plays but 50 stays out of the first 10 rounds.
The probability works out to 234/245.
ping-pong-tournament-ii.solution: Competitor $49$ is eliminated in the first $10$ rounds only by meeting $50$. Accounting for the cases where $49$ has not yet entered, or has entered while $50$ stays out, the survival probability is $\dfrac{234}{245}$ (confirmed by simulation).

hints: Integrate P(M>2y)·f_L(y) over y.
Portfolio delta = 100·0.33 = 33.
The underlying has delta 1, so hedge by selling 33 units.
Answer: −33.
simple-delta-hedge-i.solution: The $100$ calls carry delta $100\times0.33=33$. To neutralize it, sell $33$ units of the underlying (delta $1$ each), i.e. $-33.$

hints: This is the ballot problem with ties allowed (A never behind).
voter-mayhem-ii.solution: The probability that A is never behind (ties allowed) is the ballot result $Q(n,m)=\dfrac{n-m+1}{n+1}$. For $n=100,m=80$: $\dfrac{21}{101}.$

hints: Payout = +A if A>B, −B if A≤B (ties go to Bob); A~U{1..30}, B~U{1..20}.
Sum over all 600 outcomes: the A>20 block alone gives +5100.
2030-die-split-i.solution: Alice receives $A$ when $A>B$ and pays $B$ when $A\le B$ (ties to Bob). Summing over the $30\times20=600$ equally likely outcomes gives a net of $4890$, so $\mathbb{E}[\text{payout}]=\dfrac{4890}{600}=\dfrac{163}{20}=8.15.$

hints: Decreasing a face flips its parity; the total even-count averages back to 3.
P(even)=E[#even faces]/6.
dot-removal.solution: Subtracting $1$ flips one face's parity. Whether an even face (count $\to2$) or odd face (count $\to4$) is chosen, the expected number of even faces stays $3$, so $P(\text{even})=\tfrac36=\tfrac12.$

hints: The table stands iff the center (center of mass) lies inside the triangle of the 3 legs.
P(center of circle inside triangle of 3 uniform circumference points) = 1/4.
Answer: 1/4.
standing-table.solution: The table stands iff its center of mass (the disk's center) lies within the triangle formed by the three legs. For three uniform points on a circle, the center is inside their triangle with probability $\tfrac14.$

hints: To reach 4-4 the game must pass through 3-3.
Ways to reach 3-3: C(6,3)=20; then from 3-3 to 4-4 there are 2 paths (4-3→4-4 or 3-4→4-4).
tennis-deuces-ii.solution: Reaching $4$-$4$ requires first reaching $3$-$3$, which happens in $\binom63=20$ ways. From $3$-$3$, the only routes to $4$-$4$ are via $4$-$3$ or $3$-$4$ ($2$ ways). So $20\times2=40.$

hints: For a deterministic integrand, ∫₀ᵗ f(s)dB_s is N(0, ∫₀ᵗ f²ds) (Itô isometry).
Var = ∫₀ᵗ s² ds.
integral-variance-v.solution: By the Itô isometry, $\mathrm{Var}(X_t)=\int_0^t s^2\,ds=\dfrac{t^3}{3}$, so $k=\dfrac13.$

hints: Write position as a sum of 16 unit vectors in random directions.
E[|ΣVᵢ|²]=ΣE[|Vᵢ|²]+cross terms; cross terms vanish by symmetry.
skater-boy.solution: The displacement is $\sum_{i=1}^{16}V_i$ of unit vectors in independent uniform directions. Then $\mathbb{E}[|\sum V_i|^2]=\sum\mathbb{E}[|V_i|^2]+\sum_{i\ne j}\mathbb{E}[V_i\cdot V_j]=16+0=16.$

hints: P(2 gray | top)=0.4²=0.16; P(2 gray | bottom)=0.7²=0.49.
Both shelves equally likely a priori.
P(lose game n)=(n+1)/(n+2); P(W≥k)=Π telescopes to 2/(k+1).
This harmonic series diverges ⇒ infinite ⇒ −1.
gambling-addiction.solution: Losing game $n$ has probability $\tfrac{n+1}{n+2}$, so $P(W\ge k)=\prod_{n=1}^{k-1}\tfrac{n+1}{n+2}=\tfrac{2}{k+1}$. Then $\mathbb{E}[W]=\sum_{k\ge1}\tfrac{2}{k+1}=\infty$, so the answer is $-1.$

hints: Total schedules = anagrams of 111222333444555 = 15!/(3!)⁵ = 168168000.
Subtract those with the restricted student in slot 1: a fraction 3/15 of the total.
student-appointment.solution: Total schedules are anagrams of $111222333444555$: $\dfrac{15!}{(3!)^5}=168168000$. The restricted student occupies slot 1 in a fraction $\tfrac{3}{15}=\tfrac15$ of these, so the count is $168168000\cdot\tfrac45=134534400.$

hints: From each inner square, track the random up-left/up-right walk to the top row, reflecting at the side edges.
Compute the corner-reaching probability from each of the 9 starting squares.
Average over the uniform starting square.
With k eggs and d drops, the max testable floors satisfy f(d,k)=f(d−1,k−1)+f(d−1,k)+1.
This equals C(d,1)+C(d,2)+C(d,3) for 3 eggs.
Evaluate at d=9.
P(perform & Philly)=0.8·0.1=0.08; P(perform & Baltimore)=0.2·0.8=0.16.
P(Philly | perform)=0.08/(0.08+0.16).
Condition on the first 9 dice summing to x.
The last die is uniform on 1–6, hitting each residue mod 6 exactly once.
So the total is uniform mod 6 ⇒ 1/6.
divisible-dice.solution: Whatever the first nine dice sum to, the tenth die is uniform on $\{1,\dots,6\}$, taking each residue mod $6$ exactly once. Hence the total is uniform mod $6$, and the probability of divisibility is $\tfrac16.$

hints: 0.abcabc… = (100a+10b+c)/999, summed over distinct ordered (a,b,c).
Each digit position sums to 72·(0+…+9)=3240 over the 720 triples.
abc-sum.solution: Each repeating decimal equals $\tfrac{100a+10b+c}{999}$. Over the $10\cdot9\cdot8=720$ distinct ordered triples, each position's digits sum to $72(0+1+\cdots+9)=3240$, so the total is $\dfrac{111\cdot3240}{999}=360.$

hints: Stop when a draw exceeds threshold T; T solves the indifference V=E[max(x,V)]−0.02.
0.5V²−V+0.48=0 ⇒ V=0.8 (the threshold).
First draw is free, so payout=∫₀¹max(x,0.8)dx = 0.64+0.18 = 0.82.
rng-on-rng.solution: The continuation value $V$ (with the \$0.02 fee) satisfies $V=\int_0^1\max(x,V)\,dx-0.02$, i.e. $0.5V^2-V+0.48=0$, giving threshold $V=0.8$. Since the first draw is free, the expected payout is $\int_0^1\max(x,0.8)\,dx=0.64+0.18=0.82.$

hints: Equal-distance laps: average speed is the harmonic mean.
racecar-speed.solution: With equal lap distances the average speed is the harmonic mean: $\dfrac{3}{\tfrac1{100}+\tfrac1{150}+\tfrac1{200}}=\dfrac{3}{13/600}=\dfrac{1800}{13}.$

hints: For no-intercept regression, t = r√(n−1)/√(1−r²) under H₀.
significant-others.solution: The test statistic is $t=\dfrac{r\sqrt{n-1}}{\sqrt{1-r^2}}$. With $r=\tfrac1{10\sqrt3}$, $r^2=\tfrac1{300}$ and $\dfrac{r}{\sqrt{1-r^2}}=\dfrac1{\sqrt{299}}$, so $t=\sqrt{\tfrac{n-1}{299}}$. Requiring $t\ge1.645$ gives $n-1\ge1.645^2\cdot299\approx809.1$, so $n=811.$

hints: The table has a unique center point.
Play first at the exact center, then mirror every opponent move through the center.
You always have a reply ⇒ go first (answer 1).
first-quarter.solution: Place the first quarter exactly at the center, then mirror each opponent placement through the center. Every move they can make has a symmetric reply, so you never run out first — you should go first, answer $1.$

inheritance-split.solution: With Jack $=n$ and Jill $=100-n$: $\tfrac14(100-n)-\tfrac13 n=11$, i.e. $25-\tfrac{7n}{12}=11$, so $\tfrac{7n}{12}=14$ and $n=24.$

hints: Bet bᵢ so payouts equalize: 2b₁=4b₂=6b₃=R, with b₁+b₂+b₃=1.
Profit = R−1 = 1/11.
horse-arbitrage.solution: Choose bets so every outcome returns $R$: $b_1=\tfrac R2,b_2=\tfrac R4,b_3=\tfrac R6$. Then $R\big(\tfrac12+\tfrac14+\tfrac16\big)=\tfrac{11}{12}R=1$, so $R=\tfrac{12}{11}$ and the guaranteed profit is $R-1=\tfrac1{11}.$

hints: With k pairs left (2k cards), a draw removes a pair w.p. 1/(2k−1).
So removing one pair takes a geometric mean of 2k−1 turns.
card-turner.solution: When $k$ pairs remain ($2k$ cards), a random draw of two matches with probability $\dfrac{k}{\binom{2k}{2}}=\dfrac1{2k-1}$, so clearing that pair takes on average $2k-1$ turns. Summing, $\mathbb{E}[T]=\sum_{k=1}^{10}(2k-1)=100.$

hints: Mod 99: 19x₂≡19 ⇒ x₂=1, then x₁=19, x₃=80.
animal-farm.solution: Doubling the cost equation and subtracting the count equation gives $99x_1+19x_2=1900$. Reducing mod $99$, $x_2=1$, so $x_1=\tfrac{1881}{99}=19$ and $x_3=80$. Thus $x_1x_2x_3=19\cdot1\cdot80=1520.$

hints: Threshold strategy: keep all cards ≥ k; f(k)=(100+k)/2 − (k−1).
f(k)=51 − k/2 is decreasing in k.
Best is k=1 (keep everything): f(1)=101/2.
mean-cards-i.solution: Keeping all cards $\ge k$ pays the mean $\tfrac{100+k}{2}$ minus the $k-1$ discard costs: $f(k)=51-\tfrac k2$, decreasing in $k$. So keep everything ($k=1$), giving $f(1)=\tfrac{101}{2}.$

hints: Payoff = 2·1(S≥24) − 1; replicate with 2 binary calls at K=24 minus 1 bond.
binary-option.solution: The payoff is $2\cdot\mathbf 1(S_T\ge24)-1$, replicated by $2$ binary calls struck at $24$ minus one bond. The price is $2(0.73)-0.9=1.46-0.9=0.56.$

hints: Minimize the year first; months need a leading 0 or 1 and days need small digits.
Years 20XX consume 0 and 2, blocking valid all-distinct month/day combos.
The first workable year is 2345 (digits 2,3,4,5), with 06/17 → 06/17/2345.
distinct-date-i.solution: All-distinct dates require the four year digits plus a valid month and day with no repeats. Years $20\text{XX}$ tie up $0$ and $2$, leaving no room for a legal month/day, and the same obstruction rules out everything up to $2344$. The first feasible year is $2345$ (digits $2,3,4,5$), and the earliest month/day from the remaining $\{0,1,6,7,8,9\}$ is $06/17$. So the date is $06/17/2345$, i.e. $06172345.$

hints: With n sixes and 18−n threes, EV=(6n+3(18−n))/18.
Set equal to 4: 3n+54=72.
Cov(X+Y,X−Y)=Var(X)−Var(Y)=σ_X²−σ_Y².
With ρ=−1: Var(X+Y)=(σ_X−σ_Y)², Var(X−Y)=(σ_X+σ_Y)².
Corr = (σ_X²−σ_Y²)/((σ_Y−σ_X)(σ_X+σ_Y)) = −1.
